Portugal
is a dream destination brimming with stunning landscapes, vibrant culture, and delicious cuisine—but first, you need to get there!

Whether you’re flying into the bustling cities of Lisbon or Porto, driving scenic routes from neighboring countries, or even arriving by sea, there are countless ways to embark on your Portuguese adventure.

This guide will walk you through the best options to reach Portugal and help you navigate its diverse regions with ease. Ready to plan your journey? Let’s dive into how to get to Portugal and make your trip unforgettable!
How to Get to Portugal

Dreaming of exploring Portugal’s vibrant cities, stunning beaches, and rich culture? Getting there is the first step in your adventure! With a variety of transportation options—whether by air, road, or sea—you can choose the journey that best fits your travel style. Let’s dive into the most convenient and exciting ways to arrive in this beautiful country:
By Air
Flying is the quickest and most convenient way to reach Portugal, with five international airports welcoming travelers from all over the world:
Lisbon Portela Airport (LIS)
Porto Francisco Sá Carneiro Airport (OPO)
Faro Airport (FAO)
Madeira airport in Funchal (FNC)
João Paulo II Airport in Ponta Delgada (PDL)

Lisbon Portela Airport is the country’s largest airport and is well-connected to major cities worldwide. Travelers from these cities can find direct flights to Lisbon. Alternatively, travelers can also consider flying into Porto or Faro, which are served by several airlines from other cities in Europe. Accommodations near Lisbon airport are convenient for travelers with early or late flights.

For those looking to explore the islands of Portugal, there are also options to fly to the Azores and Madeira. These islands, located off the coast of Portugal, are known for their stunning natural beauty. Flights to the Azores and Madeira are available from various European cities and offer a unique travel experience.
Airlines that service Portugal from Lisbon International Airport include:
Aegean Airlines
Aer Lingus
Air Algerie
Air Arabia Maroc
Air Baltic Corp.
Air Canada
Air Europa
Air France

Getting to Portugal by road

Getting to Portugal by road offers the flexibility and freedom of a road trip, allowing travelers to explore neighboring countries and enjoy scenic routes. Portugal shares its borders with Spain, but you can also plan to get here from Spain’s neighboring countries, such as France and even Morocco (by ferry); there are several options for reaching Portugal.

Border crossings between Portugal and these countries are well-developed, making the journey seamless. However, it is essential to note that certain documentation, such as a valid passport or visa, may be required depending on your nationality.

Portugal has an extensive network of toll roads, known as “Autoestradas,” which are well-maintained and provide efficient connectivity to various regions within the country. It is essential to have the appropriate documentation and permits for using toll roads, such as an electronic toll device or a prepaid card.

For tourists traveling by car, it is crucial to familiarize themselves with Portugal’s driving rules and regulations, including speed limits and parking restrictions. Whether embarking on a road trip from a neighboring country or exploring the diverse regions within Portugal, traveling by road offers a unique and immersive experience, allowing visitors to discover the country’s hidden gems at their own pace.
Getting to Portugal by sea
Getting to Portugal by sea is a popular and convenient option for travelers. There are several options available, including ferry services and cruises.

Ferry services provide a convenient way to reach Portugal from neighboring countries such as Spain and Morocco. The ports of entry for these ferry services include Faro, Portimão, Lisbon, Porto, and Viana do Castelo. Multiple routes are available, allowing you to choose the most convenient and cost-effective option for your trip. However, specific visa requirements may depend on your passport, so enquire about these details beforehand.

Cruise ships also offer a luxurious and leisurely way to reach Portugal. Many major cruise lines include Portugal in their itineraries, making it easy to explore the country while enjoying the amenities and entertainment on board. When planning your trip to Portugal by sea, consider the options available and choose the one that best suits your preferences and schedule. Whether you opt for a ferry service or a cruise, arriving in Portugal by sea offers a unique and memorable travel experience. Please do your homework regarding the paperwork before paying for any service.
How do you get around Portugal using public transportation?

Portugal has a robust network of public transport buses and trains covering the entire country. Public transportation in Portugal is cheap, convenient, and easy to use. Besides buses and trains, you can also use the various bike and car rentals throughout the country.

Bus services in Portugal are categorized into three main types: Carreiras, Expressos and Rápidas, and Alta Qualidade. Carreiras are local bus services that connect smaller towns and villages not served by the rail network, offering an economical option for short-distance travel. Expressos and Rápidas are faster services connecting major cities and towns, balancing speed and cost. Alta Qualidade services provide the highest level of comfort and speed, often with amenities such as Wi-Fi and more spacious seating, but at a higher price. Online ticket sales are available for these services. However, travelers may need to navigate various private operators and potential scheduling limitations.
Getting around Portugal by high-speed train
Getting around Portugal
by train is an affordable and convenient way to explore the country. The train network system in Portugal is well-developed, with both regular and high-speed trains available.

Regular trains are popular for traveling between major cities and day trips to nearby destinations. They offer comfortable seating and beautiful views of the Portuguese countryside. The ticket prices for regular trains are relatively low, making it a budget-friendly option for travelers.

High-speed trains are also available for those looking for a faster mode of transportation. These trains connect major cities like Lisbon, Porto, and Faro, allowing passengers to reach their destinations quickly and comfortably. Opting for a high-speed train offers advantages over flying, such as cost-effectiveness and reduced travel time, particularly for visitors planning an itinerary that focuses on Lisbon and Porto. Although the ticket prices for high-speed trains are slightly higher than regular trains, the time saved is well worth it.

Some popular train routes in Portugal include Lisbon to Porto, which offers stunning coastal views, and Lisbon to Faro, the gateway to beautiful beaches in the Algarve region. Additionally, day trips to places like Sintra, Cascais, and Coimbra are easily accessible by train. To plan your train journey, it’s recommended to visit the
official train websites

to check train times and purchase tickets. The extensive regional train network allows you to easily navigate Portugal and explore its beautiful cities, beaches, and countryside. So hop on a train and enjoy the convenience and affordability of
traveling around Portugal
by rail.
Getting around Portugal by buses

Another option when it comes to getting around Portugal bus travel is to explore different cities and regions. The country has an extensive bus route network connecting major cities and towns, making it easy to navigate and explore.

One of the advantages of traveling by bus in Portugal is its affordability. Bus tickets are often cheaper than other transport modes, such as trains or flights. This makes it an ideal choice for budget travelers or those looking to save money while still enjoying their trip.

Additionally, buses offer a high level of convenience, as they provide direct connections to popular destinations. For example, if you’re planning to travel from Lisbon to Lagos or Braga to Faro, there are several bus routes available that can take you there without any hassle. Local services are available to connect smaller towns and villages not served by the national rail network.

The approximate costs of these bus journeys vary depending on the distance and the bus company you choose. On average, a bus ticket from Lisbon to Lagos can cost around €20-€25, while a trip from Braga to Faro may range from €15-€20. It’s important to note that prices may vary during peak travel seasons.

Overall, bus travel in Portugal is a great way to explore the country while staying within budget. Its affordable fares and convenient routes offer a reliable and efficient mode of transportation for locals and tourists alike. So sit back, relax, and enjoy the scenic views as you travel across this beautiful country by bus.
Getting around Portugal by car/bike rentals

When planning a trip to Portugal, renting a car or bike is one of the most convenient ways to get around the country. Renting a car gives you the freedom to explore at your own pace and venture into the off-beaten path. Rental cars are available in major towns and cities, providing convenience for travelers. However, we recommend you do this, keeping safety in mind.
There are several car rental companies in Portugal, and it’s best to book in advance to secure the best rates and availability. Websites like
RentalCars
can help you compare prices and find the perfect car for your trip.

If you prefer a more eco-friendly option or want to experience Portugal from a different perspective, bike rentals are also available in major cities and tourist areas. Riding a bike allows you to immerse yourself in the local culture and enjoy the beautiful scenery.

Consider flying into Porto and out of Faro to make your trip more convenient. This way, you can explore the northern part of the country, including the vibrant city of Porto with its delicious food scene and iconic landmarks, such as the Ribeira district and
Dom Luís I Bridge
. From Porto, you can also take a day trip to the stunning Douro Valley and enjoy wine tastings and breathtaking views of the vineyards.

In Faro, you can relax on the beautiful beaches of the Algarve region or explore the charming old town with its narrow streets and historic buildings. Renting a car or bike in both cities will allow you to make the most of your time and experience Portugal’s diverse attractions and landscapes.
If you plan to get a bike or car rental on your next trip to Portugal, ensure you have a valid license and all other necessary documents.
Best Time to Visit Portugal
Portugal can be enjoyed throughout all seasons, so the best time to visit Portugal will depend entirely on your preferences and the purpose of your visit.
The seasons of spring, which lasts from March to May, and autumn, which lasts from September to October, are highly recommended for visiting Portugal. You will find
fewer crowds
and enjoy mild temperatures during this time.

Witness the blooming landscapes and the re-opening of the many attractions in Portugal during Spring as the quieter cold months end. During autumn, you can explore Portugal’s countryside and the plethora of winery activities Portugal has to offer.
However, if you don’t mind the cold months, you can visit Portugal in December and January and enjoy the festive festivities of the season.
If you are visiting
Portugal to surf
along the
many surf spots
it has to offer, the ideal time to visit would be between
May and September
for
beginner surfers
and between September and April for experienced surfers.
No matter when you choose to visit Portugal,
Portugal has a lot of things to offer all year round

. From the stunning beaches along the coast to the charming cities like Lisbon and Porto, there is plenty to see and do. So, plan your trip accordingly and make the most of your time in this beautiful country.
Weather and Climate

Portugal boasts a Mediterranean climate characterized by mild winters and warm summers, making it a year-round destination. However, the weather can vary significantly depending on the region. The north tends to be cooler and wetter, while the south enjoys more sunshine and higher temperatures.

The best times to visit Portugal are spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the temperatures are pleasant, ranging from 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F), and the tourist crowds are thinner, allowing for a more relaxed experience. Spring brings blooming landscapes and vibrant festivals, while autumn offers a chance to explore the countryside and participate in winery activities.

Summer (June to August) can be pretty hot, especially in the southern regions like the Algarve, with temperatures often exceeding 30°C (86°F). This is the perfect time for beach lovers to soak up the sun and enjoy the coastal beauty. However, be prepared for larger crowds and higher prices during this peak tourist season.

Winter (December to February) is generally mild, with temperatures ranging from 10°C to 15°C (50°F to 59°F). While it may be cooler, it’s still a great time to explore Portugal’s cities and enjoy the festive atmosphere. Pack some layers for the cooler mornings and evenings, and don’t forget your umbrella, as winter can bring rain.

No matter when you visit, Portugal’s weather can be unpredictable, so it’s wise to pack layers and be prepared for sudden changes in temperature or precipitation. Sunscreen and a hat are essential for sunny days, while a light jacket will keep you comfortable during cooler evenings.
Food in Portugal

Get ready for a delightful journey that Portuguese cuisine will take you on when you are here. Portuguese cuisine leans more towards seafood and meat-based dishes, showcasing the richness of their local produce and traditional cooking techniques. This means that vegetarian and vegan travelers may sometimes need help finding something that suits their dietary preferences. However, having said that finding
good plant-based meals in Portugal
is not impossible as the options are growing.

One must-try treat in Portugal is the iconic Pastel de Nata, a delicious custard tart with a crispy puff pastry shell. These delectable pastries often enjoyed with a sprinkle of cinnamon and powdered sugar, can be found in bakeries and cafes across the country.

Another popular dish to savor is Migas, a comforting blend of bread, garlic, olive oil, and regional spices. This traditional dish varies from region to region, offering a unique taste experience wherever you go.

To add a bit of sweetness and kick to your culinary exploration, make sure to try Ginja liqueur. This cherry liqueur is typically served in small chocolate cups and is commonly enjoyed as an aperitif or digestive. Whether you indulge in the meat and fish-based dishes that Portugal is renowned for or opt for the vegetarian-friendly options available, exploring the country’s rich and diverse culinary traditions is a journey worth savoring.
Accommodation in Portugal
Since Portugal is a hot tourist destination, you will find a variety of options. From luxury hotels to cozy guesthouses to
surf
camps in Portugal, there is something to suit every traveler’s needs.

Tourists can easily find hotels as they are widely available throughout the country in a diverse range of cost brackets – from budget-friendly options to high-end rooms. Hotels offer convenience and amenities, especially in major cities and popular tourist areas. Private rentals like apartments and villas provide a more intimate and home-like experience.
There are various booking platforms that can be used to find the perfect place to stay in Portugal.
Booking.com

is one of the most popular websites, offering a wide range of options and competitive prices. Platforms like Airbnb and HomeAway also provide private rentals for those seeking a more unique and personalized experience.

If you are visiting during the cold months, we recommend that you enquire if your accommodation has heating options as the temperature can fall quite a bit during the nights in cold months. Irrespective of that, conduct thorough research into the places you are booking to ensure that all the amenities you seek are available.
Currency in Portugal

The official currency accepted throughout Portugal is the Euro, from the bustling city centers to the countryside. If you need to exchange your currency for Euro, you can easily find currency exchange offices in major cities and tourist areas. Just ask, “Where is the nearest currency exchange office?” and the locals will be happy to help. Credit cards are widely accepted in most establishments, so you can conveniently pay with your card.

When planning your trip to Portugal, knowing some basic phrases related to currency exchange is always helpful. Here are a few examples: “Onde fica o câmbio mais próximo?” (Where is the nearest currency exchange?) and “Posso pagar com cartão de crédito?” (Can I pay with a credit card?). With the Euro as Portugal’s currency, you can enjoy a hassle-free trip, knowing you’ll have no trouble using your money throughout the country.
Top 5 Cities to Visit in Portugal
We are not saying that there are only 5 cities that are worth visiting when traveling to Portugal, but if you are short on time, here are the 5 cities you should not miss:
1. Porto
Situated along the Douro River, Porto is the second-largest city in Portugal. It is known for its charming and colorful houses, medieval streets, and exquisite port wine.
Vila Nova

de Gaia, located across the Douro River, is known for its port lodges and stunning views of Porto. Visit the Ribeira district, explore the riverside promenade, or take a boat tour to admire the incredible architecture. Take advantage of the iconic
Clerigos Tower
or the beautiful
Livraria Lello bookstore
, which is said to have inspired J.K. Rowling’s Harry Potter series.
2. Sintra
Sintra

is famous for its fairytale-like Pena Palace, an enchanting blend of architectural styles, vibrant colors, and small forests. Other must-see attractions include the Moorish Castle and the mysterious Initiation Well at Quinta da Regaleira. Stroll through the historic center to discover charming cafés and boutique shops.
3. Coimbra

As Portugal’s university city, Coimbra offers a unique blend of youthful energy and rich history. Explore the University of Coimbra, one of the oldest universities in the world, and visit the stunning
Joanina Library

. Take a walk through the picturesque streets of the historic center and marvel at the intricate azulejo tiles. For a tranquil experience, visit the breathtaking Mondego River and enjoy the panoramic views.
4. Faro

Located in the sunny Algarve region, Faro offers beautiful beaches and captivating coastal views. Explore the historic center with its charming cobblestone streets and medieval walls. Visit the beautiful
Faro Cathedral
and enjoy panoramic views from its tower. Take a boat trip to the nearby Ria Formosa Natural Park, a paradise for bird-watching and nature lovers.
5. Évora

A UNESCO World Heritage Site, Évora is a charming and well-preserved medieval city. Stroll through the narrow streets of the historic center and admire the impressive Roman Temple. Visit the bone chapel, known as Capela dos Ossos, which has its macabre but fascinating interior covered in human skulls and bones. Don’t miss the stunning Évora Cathedral or the picturesque Giraldo Square. Each of these cities in Portugal offers unique experiences and attractions, making them must-visit destinations for any traveler.
5 Best Things to Do in Portugal

Portugal is a country rich in history, culture, and natural beauty. From the vibrant city of Lisbon to the stunning coastal areas, there is no shortage of things to do and see in this remarkable destination. Here are some top activities to include in your itinerary:
1. Immerse yourself in the culture of Lisbon

Begin your ultimate trip to Portugal by exploring Lisbon’s vibrant capital city. Lisbon’s Santa Apolonia station is a key arrival point, well-connected to the city’s metro system. Wander through the charming streets of Alfama, the oldest district, and listen to the melancholic melodies of Fado music, a traditional genre deeply rooted in Portuguese culture. Discover iconic landmarks like the Jerónimos Monastery, which showcase the country’s rich history. Don’t miss the chance to indulge in the delectable local cuisine, sampling pastéis de nata (custard tarts) and fresh seafood dishes.
2. Embark on an ‘Age of Discovery’ tour at a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Feel the spirit of Portugal’s maritime past by visiting historic sites connected to the Age of Discovery. Head to Belém, where you can explore the Monument to the Discoveries, a tribute to the country’s famous explorers. Visit the awe-inspiring Belém Tower, a fortress overlooking the Tagus River, and step inside the majestic Mosteiro dos Jerónimos, a symbol of Portugal’s golden era.
3. Explore Sintra, the castle town of Portugal

Just a short train ride from Lisbon lies the enchanting town of Sintra, a UNESCO World Heritage site dotted with fairytale-like castles and palaces. Marvel at the colorful façade of the Pena Palace, perched on a hilltop, and wander through the lush gardens of Quinta da Regaleira. Don’t miss the opportunity to try the local specialty, queijadas de Sintra, a delicious cheesecake enjoyed for centuries.
4. Discover the pristine beaches

Portugal is renowned for its stunning coastline and beautiful beaches. Visit the picturesque town of Cascais, situated on the Atlantic Ocean, and soak up the sun on its sandy shores. Spending a few hours at the beach is a great way to relax and enjoy Portugal’s stunning coastline. For a more adventurous beach experience, head to the Algarve region, known for its hidden beaches and dramatic cliffs. Explore the breathtaking rock formations in Ponta da Piedade and relax on the pristine sand of Praia da Marinha, often regarded as one of the most beautiful beaches in the world.
5. Surf your heart out in Portugal.
Portugal is a surfer’s paradise
with its consistent waves and ideal weather. Whether you’re a beginner or an experienced surfer, plenty of
surf camps
and schools along the coast cater to your needs. From the famous breaks of
Ericeira
to the laid-back vibe of Sagres, you’ll find
surf spots
to suit every level.
Experience the thrill of riding the waves and immerse yourself in Portugal’s vibrant
surf culture

. In a nutshell, Portugal offers a myriad of activities for every traveler. From soaking in the history and culture of Lisbon to exploring fairy tale castles in Sintra, and from basking on stunning beaches to riding the waves along the coast, a trip to Portugal is an incredible adventure filled with unforgettable experiences.

UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Portugal

Portugal is home to 17 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, each a testament to the country’s rich cultural and historical heritage. These sites offer a glimpse into Portugal’s past and are must-visit destinations for any traveler.
The Historic Centre of Porto
: Wander through the medieval streets of Porto, a city known for its charming architecture and vibrant atmosphere. Take advantage of the iconic Ribeira district along the Douro River.
The Tower of Belém in Lisbon
: This stunning fortress overlooking the Tagus River symbolizes Portugal’s Age of Discovery. Its intricate Manueline architecture is a sight to behold.
The Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon
: Another masterpiece of Manueline architecture, this monastery is a tribute to Portugal’s maritime explorers. The ornate details and grand cloisters are genuinely awe-inspiring.
The Pena Palace in Sintra
: Perched on a hilltop, this colorful palace looks like something out of a fairy tale. Its eclectic mix of architectural styles and lush gardens make it a must-visit.
The Moorish Castle in Sintra
: Explore the ancient ruins of this castle, which offers panoramic views of the surrounding landscape. It’s a great spot for history buffs and nature lovers alike.
The Historic Centre of Évora
: Stroll through the well-preserved medieval streets of Évora, a city rich in history. Highlights include the Roman Temple and the macabre Capela dos Ossos (Chapel of Bones).
The Convent of Christ in Tomar
: This former Templar stronghold is a fascinating blend of Gothic, Manueline, and Renaissance architecture. The intricate details and historical significance make it a must-see.
The Monastery of Batalha
: A masterpiece of Gothic architecture, this monastery was built to commemorate the Battle of Aljubarrota. Its stunning stained glass windows and intricate stonework are breathtaking.
The Prehistoric Rock Art Sites in the Côa Valley
: Discover ancient rock carvings that date back thousands of years. These sites offer a unique glimpse into prehistoric life and art.
The Landscape of the Pico Island Vineyard Culture
: Located in the Azores, this UNESCO site showcases the unique vineyard culture of Pico Island. The volcanic landscape and traditional wine-making methods are fascinating to explore.
The Garrison Border Town of Elvas and its Fortifications
: This well-preserved fortress town offers a glimpse into Portugal’s military history. The star-shaped fortifications and historic buildings are impressive.
The University of Coimbra – Alta and Sofia
: One of the oldest universities in the world, Coimbra’s university is a hub of learning and culture. The stunning Joanina Library is a highlight.
The Royal Building of Mafra – Palace, Basilica, Convent, Cerco Garden, and Hunting Park (Tapada)
: This grand complex is a testament to Portugal’s royal history. The opulent palace and serene gardens are worth exploring.
The Sanctuary of Bom Jesus do Monte in Braga
: This baroque sanctuary is known for its stunning staircase and beautiful views. It’s a place of pilgrimage and a symbol of faith.

These UNESCO World Heritage Sites are a testament to Portugal’s rich cultural and historical heritage. Include them in your itinerary for a deeper understanding of the country’s past and architectural marvels.
Safety and Accessibility
Portugal is generally a safe country to visit, with lower crime rates than other European destinations. However, taking standard precautions is always wise to ensure a safe and enjoyable trip.
Safety Tips for Travelers in Portugal
Be aware of your surroundings
: In crowded areas and tourist hotspots, stay vigilant and monitor your belongings.
Keep valuables secure
: Consider using a money belt or a secure bag to keep your valuables safe.
Avoid walking alone in dimly lit areas at night
: Stick to well-lit and populated areas to ensure your safety.
Use licensed taxis or ride-sharing services
: Always check the driver’s ID before getting in, and avoid unlicensed taxis.
Be mindful of pickpocketing and petty theft
: Keep your belongings close in crowded areas and be cautious of your surroundings.
Avoid displaying signs of wealth
: Refrain from wearing expensive jewelry or watches that might attract unwanted attention.
Keep your hotel room door locked
: Use the hotel safe, if available, to store your valuables.
Be cautious when using ATMs
: Avoid using ATMs in isolated areas, and be aware of your surroundings when withdrawing cash.
Accessible Transportation in Portugal

Portugal has made significant strides in improving accessibility for visitors with disabilities. Many public buildings, including museums, restaurants, and hotels, are equipped with wheelchair ramps and accessible toilets. The public transportation system is also relatively accessible, with many options available for travelers with disabilities.
Buses and Trains

: Many bus companies, including Rede Expressos and Rodonorte, have accessible buses with ramps and wheelchair spaces. The train network also offers accessible services, with staff trained to assist passengers with disabilities.
Metro Systems
: The metro systems in Lisbon and Porto are equipped with elevators and ramps, making it easier for travelers with disabilities to navigate the platforms.
Accessible Taxis
: Many taxi companies offer accessible vehicles, and some cities have dedicated accessible taxi services. It’s advisable to book these services in advance to ensure availability.
MyWay Service

: This personalized assistance service is available for passengers with disabilities on public transportation. It can be booked in advance through the transportation company’s website or by contacting them directly.

While Portugal is relatively accessible, it’s always a good idea to research and plan ahead to ensure you can access the places you want to visit. With the right preparation, travelers with disabilities can enjoy a comfortable and enjoyable trip to Portugal.

By following these safety tips and taking advantage of the accessible transportation options, you can have a safe and enjoyable trip to Portugal, exploring all the beauty and culture this country has to offer.
10 Things to Know for Your Trip to Portugal (summarized)
Planning a trip to Portugal? Here are the top 10 things you should know and not miss on your trip to Portugal:
1. Travel Insurance:
Purchasing travel insurance before your trip is crucial. This ensures you are covered for any unexpected events or emergencies.
2. Lisbon:
Visit Portugal’s capital city, Lisbon
. Explore the charming neighborhoods, indulge in delicious cuisine, and enjoy the vibrant nightlife.
3. Sintra:
Don’t miss the enchanting town of Sintra, known for its fairytale-like palaces, such as the famous Pena Palace. Take a day trip from Lisbon to experience its magical atmosphere.
4. Benagil Caves:
Discover the stunning Benagil caves in the Algarve region. These natural wonders can only be accessed by boat and offer breathtaking views.
5. Costa Nova Beach:
Head to Costa Nova Beach near Aveiro to admire its colorful striped houses and relax on its pristine sandy shores.
6. Porto:
Explore the vibrant city of Porto, renowned for its port wine production. Visit the cellars, stroll along the Douro River, and soak up the city’s rich history.
7. Douro Valley:
Take a trip to the picturesque Douro Valley, known for its vineyards and scenic landscapes. Enjoy wine tastings and river cruises as you explore this beautiful region.
8. Porto Wine:
Indulge in the world-famous Porto wine and discover its unique flavors. Take a tour of the cellars and learn about their production process.
9. Fado Music:
Experience the soul-stirring sounds of Fado music, Portugal’s traditional music genre. Enjoy live performances at local bars and restaurants in Lisbon and Porto.
10. Pastel de Nata:

Don’t leave Portugal without trying the delicious Pastel de Nata, a famous Portuguese custard tart. Sample these sweet treats at local bakeries throughout the country. Whether you’re exploring Lisbon’s narrow streets, getting lost in the beauty of Sintra, or savoring the flavors of Porto, Portugal offers a diverse range of experiences that will leave you wanting to return for more.

FAQs
What is the best time to visit Portugal?
Portugal is a year-round destination, but the spring (April to June) and fall (September to October) offer pleasant weather with fewer crowds. Summer (July to August) is ideal for beach lovers.
How can I
get around in Portugal

Portugal has an efficient public transportation system, including trains and buses. Renting a car is a popular option for exploring more remote areas, and major cities also have well-connected metro systems. Nearly all metro stations in Porto are accessible for wheelchair users, with ramps, elevators, and dedicated spaces on trains.
Are there visa requirements for visiting Portugal?

Portugal is part of the Schengen Area, and travelers from many countries, including the US and EU member states, do not need a visa for short stays (up to 90 days). Check visa requirements based on your nationality.
What are the must-visit places in Portugal?
Explore the historic charm of Lisbon, the picturesque Douro Valley, the Algarve’s coastal beauty, and Porto’s cultural richness. Each region offers a unique experience.
Is English widely spoken in Portugal?
While Portuguese is the official language, English is widely spoken, especially in tourist areas. Most locals in the hospitality industry understand and communicate in English.
What outdoor activities are popular in Portugal?
Portugal offers diverse outdoor activities, including
surfing
, hiking, wine tasting in the Douro Valley, and exploring historic sites. The Algarve is known for its stunning beaches and water activities.
Are there surfing opportunities in Portugal?
Yes, Portugal is a renowned surf destination with consistent waves along the coast. Popular surf spots include
Ericeira
, Peniche, and the Algarve. Surf schools and camps cater to all skill levels.
Are surf camps in Portugal suitable for all skill levels?

Yes, surf camps in Portugal cater to surfers of all levels, from beginners to advanced. Experienced instructors provide lessons, and the diverse coastline offers a variety of waves suitable for different skill levels.
What is the best time of year to
book a surf camp in Portugal
The
surf season in Portugal
typically runs from September to April, with the autumn and winter months providing optimal conditions for larger
swells
. However, surf camps operate year-round, offering lessons and experiences tailored to seasonal conditions.

Portugal is a country known for its rich history, stunning landscapes, and vibrant culture. From the charming streets of Lisbon to the breath-taking views of the westernmost point of Europe, there is something for everyone in this diverse and captivating destination. With so many incredible attractions and activities, it can be challenging to narrow down your itinerary. In this article, we have curated a list of  the 7 places to visit and things to do in Portugal, ensuring that you make the most of your trip to Portugal.
1. Bairro Alto

Dubbed as the party district of Lisbon, there is a whole lot more than the crowded nights and plethora of bars to this place. Bairro Alto is your crash course in learning about Portugal’s history and quaint quality of life during the day and witnessing some of the best night scenes you have witnessed. When you are here, don’t miss out on visiting the upper part of the district in the rustic Glória funicular.
Ever since its urbanization in the 15
th

century, Bairro Alto has been one of the top regions in Lisbon and the country. Today, it has an array of restaurants offering everything from comforting Portuguese culinary delights like Pastel de Nata to intercontinental cuisines.

The art scene in Bairro Alto is also unmatched.  Make sure you don’t miss the stunning murals as you walk around the cobbled stone streets and various parts of the district. Some places where you can check out amazing local street art would include Rua da Vinha, Rua de São Boaventura and Calçada da Glória.

Spend some time at the Miradouro São Pedro de Alcântara viewpoint to soak in the breath-taking views of the city. In the night, don’t miss out on watching a fado performance with a glass of port wine at one of the many bars here. You can also enjoy the performances at
Fado In Chiado theatre.
2. Park and National Palace of Pena

Get transported into the era of romanticism as you ascend to the top of the Sintra Hills to visit the Park and National Palace of Pena. The magnificence of the National Palace of Pena just cannot be missed if you are in Portugal.

Barely half an hour from Lisbon, this UNESCO World Heritage Site is as idyllic as it gets. While you are here, make sure to go take in the beauty of the park as well where you can enjoy this beautifully planned garden. The
ticket

for the park also gives you access to the Chalet and Garden of the Countess of Edla, Valley of Lakes and Little Birds Fountain, and Temple of Columns, The Warrior Statue and the Queen’s Table. The Park and National Palace of Pena is well-connected from Lisbon via public transportation.
3. Sado Estuary

Nestled along Portugal’s west coast, the Sado Estuary unveils a natural wonder beyond the bustling cities and coastal panoramas. It is a nature reserve in Portugal protected along with 30 other areas in Portugal. As a designated natural reserve, it shelters a diverse array of plant and animal species, offering a haven for birdwatchers with its flamboyant flamingos and graceful herons. If you choose to embark on a boat tour, you may get a chance to witness the resident bottlenose dolphins leaping alongside, a thrilling encounter that makes for unforgettable memories.  Nature enthusiasts can venture along the estuary’s hiking tails, winding through marshes and mudflats, or explore the traditional fishing villages like Comporta and Carrasqueira, where time seems to stand still amid colorful boats and local routines.
4. Serra de Estrela Natural Park

The Serra de Estrela is another one of the 30 areas under protection by the government and is the largest one among them all. No matter what part of the year you visit Portugal, this 101-thousand-hectare natural park is ideal to visit during all seasons. Serra de Estrela is also the highest mountain peak in mainland Portugal. You will find rustic villages, scenic lakes, granite outcrops and an immensely diverse range of flora and fauna here. Go hiking up the Torre Mountain or from Manteigas to the Poço de Inferno.

If you are visiting between November to March, go skiing in Serra de Estrela, the only place in the country where you can indulge in this sports. If physical activity is not up your alley and you are looking for something else, you can learn about cheese-making in Pêraboa. The Museu do Queijo, a cheese museum here will leave you fascinated with the history, process and the products from raw sheep’s milk.
5. UNESCO Heritage Wine Regions

The Azores and Douro Valley in Portugal are UNESCO protected wine regions which should be on your list even if you don’t drink wine! Visit these sites for its historic significance if not for the wine.

The Archipelago of Azores is home to Pico Island, which has attracted several wine enthusiasts globally. The vineyards on this volcanic island is characterized by a giant maze-like design of black lava-stone walls. These walls have been standing strong protecting the grapes.

The Douro Valley is another must-visit place in Portugal. It is one of the oldest and culturally rich wine regions in Portugal. The world famous Port Wine gets its name from winemaking practices here that are several centuries old and derived from the practice of fortifying wine with brandy to maintain its quality.
6. Jerónimos Monastery

The Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon is yet another UNESCO World Heritage Site in Portugal. The monastery is situated along with the Tower of Belém. Also referred to as the Mosteiro dos Jerónimos in Portuguese, this is a tourist favorite built in the late Gothic style whose architecture marvels every visitor. Apart from its impressive architecture, this landmark is also important as the tomb of Vasco de Gama is housed here in the Church of Santa Maria. This church also has a unique architecture characterized by six sculpted columns that hold up the only nave in the structure.
If you are visiting Mosteiro dos Jerónimos, you can also explore the nearby Archaeology Museum.
7. Cabo Da Roca

Set foot in the westernmost point in continental Europe when you visit Cabo Da Roca. Also known as Cape Roca in English and Focinha Da Rosa, this place is close to the Sintra National Park. During the olden days, Portuguese explorers considered this place to be the end of the world. This is good place if you are looking forward to a nice and relaxed day. Here you can enjoy nice scenic views of the ocean, the sight of the Cabo Da Roca lighthouse, and explore the beaches around the place. Cabo Da Roca is often the final point or the starting point for those going hiking in the Sintra National Park.

It is a great place to relax and watch the waves crash onto the shore, especially at sunset. Time your visit perfectly and you can enjoy an unforgettable view at the edge of the continent! Along the way don’t miss the Praia Grande where tubular wave between 1 and 4 metres are not uncommon. Practice your skills or admire the pro surfers as they strut their stuff.

body.alt: View of Cabo da Roca lighthouse

children.text: You can club the visit to Cabo Da Roca with your visits Sintra or Cascais.
8. Ericeira

Known as the Canggu of Europe, Ericeira has some of the most stunning beaches of Portugal. The town is extremely popular for surfing. Even if you don’t surf, you are surely going to fall in love with the charm of this Portuguese town. Visitors can expect to the eat best Portuguese seafood cuising with produce that is as fresh as it gets. Fishing is an integral part of this region and not just for its cuisine. It is an important economic activity in the region.

If surfing piques your interest but you don’t know how to, join our surf camp in Ericeira for some beginner surf lessons. Rapture Surfcamps offers lessons for every one from beginner to advanced surfers at 3 locations in Portugal. Get theory lessons, sumptuous dinner, free Wifi, airport transfers and so much more when you choose to stay with us.
Most of our guests fly into Lisbon Airport directly, where we will pick you up in our airport shuttle.
This can be added to your booking for an extra cost, but you will be taken directly from the airport to the surf camp!
It is also possible to catch a bus to Ericeira from the Campo Grande bus station in Lisbon. We will be happy to pick you up at the bus station in Ericeira, just give us a call.

VISIT THE CAMP
Surfing

has a rich and storied history in Portugal that dates back to the early 1920s but often remains hidden beyond Portugal’s borders. While countries like Australia, the USA, Brazil, and South Africa are widely celebrated for their surf legacies, Portugal’s unique surfing heritage remains somewhat of a secret.
Here, the Atlantic Ocean shapes some of the best waves on Earth, sculpting a coastline that draws surfers from every corner of the globe.

But make no mistake—Portugal’s surf culture and talent deserve global recognition. The country is home to legendary waves and world-class surfers like Pedro Lima, Tiago Pires, Joana Rocha, and Nic Von Rupp, who have made their mark on the international stage over the decades.
So, join us as we dive into the fascinating history of
surfing in Portugal
, exploring the events and icons that have fuelled its vibrant surf culture and made it a wave-riding powerhouse.
Let’s dive in!
1926 – The first wave riders in Europe

body.alt: Old film showing first known evidence of surfing in Portugal
body.caption: The original Portuguese surf gang

children.text: France is often celebrated as the birthplace of surfing in continental Europe, tracing back to 1956 when Peter Viertel and Georges Hennebutte famously began catching waves in Biarritz.
But many don’t realize that wave riding in Europe might have started even earlier—in Portugal, as far back as 1926.

A fascinating discovery from the National Archive of Moving Images in Bucelas offers a glimpse into this history. There’s a 28-second black-and-white film showing a group of men bodyboarding in the whitewash at Leça da Palmeira, near Porto. In the footage, they ride on board-like crafts, skillfully navigating the
swell
along the Portuguese coast on what seems to have been a lively day of waves.
1959 – The father of surfing in Portugal

body.alt: Surfers riding waves in Portugal in the 50s
body.caption: Safe to say Pedro Lima started a bit of a trend in Portugal

children.text: Pedro Martins de Lima is celebrated as the pioneer of surfing in Portugal. This legendary waterman helped establish surfing as a beloved pastime in this country.

Pedro Martins de Lima’s journey into the world of surfing began in 1946 when he started bodysurfing—a style of riding waves without a surfboard, relying instead on the body to glide across the water. At that time, he used a pair of Churchill fins, a type of swim fin that allowed him to navigate the ocean with ease and speed. These fins were popular among watermen for better movement in the sea. They were even used by famed ocean explorer Jacques Cousteau.

In 1959, after years of bodysurfing, Pedro took the next step. He stood up on a surfboard for the first time, officially embracing the sport he would go on to pioneer in Portugal. This milestone began a lifelong passion and solidified his place in surf history.

Pedro remains a vibrant ambassador of Portuguese surf culture, working closely with the iconic brand Lightning Bolt. His incredible life has even been captured in a TV documentary, “The Wolf of the Sea,” showcasing his adventures and influence on the sport.
1987 – The birth of the national surf team

body.alt: Algarve beach Praia do Amado
body.caption: With waves like this, it was only a matter of time before surfing in Portugal became a national pastime.

children.text: After the fall of the Estado Novo regime, Portugal began welcoming sports from around the world, including surfing. Within a decade, surfing had surged in popularity, captivating both young and old, sparking the creation of Portugal’s first National Surf Team.

They soon represented the nation at the Eurosurf European Surfing Championships in France, finishing in an impressive 4th place, earning the respect of the global surfing community. This milestone ushered in an era of competitive surfing in Portugal, setting the stage for the country’s surfing stars of the 1990s.
1988 – Portuguese Surfing Federation founded

body.alt: The famous Guincho Beach in Cascais
body.caption: Guincho Beach… only a hop, skip and a jump from the FPS’s headquarters in Cascais

children.text: After Portugal’s impressive 4th place finish at the Eurosurf event in France, the nation’s surfing scene began to thrive. This success ignited discussions among the surf community about creating a dedicated governing body, which soon led to the founding of the Portuguese Surfing Federation (Federação Portuguesa de Surf, FPS).

Located in Carcavelos, Cascais, FPS is at the heart of Portugal’s surfing development. The federation focuses on training young talent to help them excel competitively. Also, it plays a crucial role in organizing surf camps to promote surfing across the country.
That’s in addition to:
Coordinating competitions
Enforcing discipline to ensure fair judging
Advocating for environmental conservation
1989 – ASP World Tour event in Portugal

body.alt: Professional surfer Rob Bain on a wave
body.caption: Rob “Bainy” Bain putting on a forehand carve clinic back in the day

children.text: The ASP (Association of Surfing Professionals) World Tour, the original World Surf League, was founded by Ian Cairns in 1983 with a mission to showcase the world’s best surfers competing on the most thrilling waves across the globe.
In 1989, the ASP World Tour held its first-ever event in Portugal—a landmark competition won by Australian surfer Rob Bain, drawing over 2,000 enthusiastic spectators to the beach.
This event marked a major milestone for the ASP and cemented Portugal’s reputation as a world-class surfing destination.
1991 – National Competition Held With Prize Money

body.alt: Portuguese surfer at a competition in France
body.caption: The surfing in Portugal by this time was already considered to be world class

children.text: Portugal had firmly established itself as a key player in the European surf scene, so the next step was to elevate its national competitions by offering prizes to the top surfers.
In 1991, this vision came to life with the launch of the first official Portuguese surfing competition, where winners were awarded cash prizes.

The champion of this landmark event was Bruno “Bubba” Charneca, a local surfer from Caparica who has since become a national icon. Known for his smooth style and relentless drive, Charneca’s performances made waves as he relentlessly fought to win every heat.
1996 – Portugal Hosted The World Championship Tour

body.alt: Kelly Slater surfing in Portugal
body.caption: Any surfer who can beat this man deserves a place in history

children.text: In 1996, the World Championship Tour (now known as the World Surf League) held the Coca-Cola Figueira event at Figueira da Foz. The lineup included three-time world champion Kelly Slater, along with other surf legends like Shane Dorian, Matt Hoy, Rob Machado, and Luke Egan. The event set several big wave records, underscoring Portugal’s importance on the global surfing map.
Among the competitors was local hero Bruno Charneca, who faced Slater in Round 2 and made history by defeating him—the first Portuguese surfer ever to eliminate the iconic Floridian.
Although Charneca’s run ended in the next round with a loss to Sunny Garcia, the competition concluded with Matt Hoy taking the win.
1998 – First female surf meeting takes place

body.alt: Female surfers in Portugal jumping into the waves
body.caption: Thanks to Joana Rocha, there’s no shortage of young female talent coming through the ranks in Portugal

children.text: In 1998, surfing in Portugal began to diversify. Alongside a strong male circuit, women started making waves of their own, leading to the rise of Portugal’s first professional female surfer, Joana Rocha.

She took her impact further by organizing female-only meetups, creating a supportive space for women in surfing. This initiative allowed Rocha to track and promote women’s involvement in the sport, inspiring the 125 female surfers active at that time to push their limits.
Today, there are over 450 female surfers in Portugal, with numbers rising every year—all sparked by Joana Rocha’s pioneering steps in 1998.
2007 – Portugal’s maiden WCT surfer

body.alt: Portuguese surfer Tiago Pires
body.caption: Tiago enjoyed strong performances in waves of consequence throughout the world until a knee injury hobbled his competitive career

children.text: Tiago “Saca” Pires was Portugal’s first surfer to compete in the prestigious World Championship Tour (WCT), the highest level of competitive surfing. He earned this spot in 2007 by reaching 5th place on the World Qualifying Series (WQS) leaderboard, a ranking system that determines who advances to the WCT.

In his first year on the WCT, Tiago placed 31st, which allowed him to continue competing at this top level. He steadily improved over the next two years, achieving a personal best of 21st place by 2010.
Unfortunately, a knee injury in 2013 interrupted his progress, and after competing in the WQS again in 2015, Tiago decided to retire from competitive surfing.
2011 – Ericiera becomes a World Surfing Reserve

body.alt: World Surfing Reserve in Ericeira
body.caption: It was only a matter of time before Ericeira was recognised as a world-class surf destination

children.text: Ericeira is Portugal’s surfing capital. Here, surf culture shapes the local landscape, economy, and community. Ericeira’s world-class waves led the Save The Waves Coalition, a global nonprofit dedicated to protecting surf ecosystems, to designate it as a World Surfing Reserve in 2011. This prestigious title not only celebrates Ericeira’s unique surf quality but also implements strategies to protect and manage its coastline, ensuring the preservation of both its
surf breaks
and the surrounding natural environment for future generations.
This Surfing Reserve spans 4 kilometers and includes iconic breaks like Pedra Branca, Reef, Ribeira d’Ilhas, Cave, Crazy Left, Coxos, and São Lourenço.
Today, Ericeira is one of only 11 World Surfing Reserves globally, joining renowned destinations like Punta de Lobos in Chile, Manly Beach in Australia, and Malibu in the United States.
2014 – First Portuguese junior world champion in surfing

body.alt: Portuguese pro surfer Vasco Ribeiro
body.caption: Vasco putting his board at the 12 O’clock mark in a local contest

children.text: Portugal’s surf scene hit a new high when Vasco Ribeiro, a young surfer from Cascais, became the country’s first junior world champion. He won this title by competing against top surfers, including Italo Ferreira, now a star in the World Surf League, in the challenging waves of Ribeira D’Ilhas in Ericeira.

This win marked a pivotal moment, but Ribeiro’s individual accomplishments made 2014 truly unforgettable. That year, he also clinched his third national title and the European junior title, showcasing his skill and solidifying Portugal’s place as a top-tier surf nation.
2017 – Biggest wave ever ridden at Nazaré

body.alt: Massive wave breaking at Nazaré
body.caption: Just the thought of getting mowed down by one of these behemoths makes us break out into a cold sweat

children.text: Just north of Lisbon, Nazaré is a legendary beach break known worldwide for its massive winter swells, often towering over 50 feet. This big-wave playground attracts the world’s bravest surfers, and in November 2017, a massive surge drew Brazilian surfer Rodrigo Koxa to its waters.
When his moment came, Koxa seized it, riding an awe-inspiring 80-foot wave—the largest wave ever documented, surpassing the previous 78-foot record set by
Garrett McNamara
at Nazaré in 2011.
Koxa’s historic ride earned him the Big Wave Surf Award for the world’s largest wave in 2018, solidifying Nazaré’s reputation as the world’s premier big-wave destination.
Surf Culture in Portugal
That brings us to the end of our timeline! But, while we’re on the subject of Portugal’s surfing history, we would be remiss not to take a closer look at Portugal’s surf culture.

Since the 1960s, a vibrant surf community has flourished, blending deep-rooted Portuguese customs with modern influences. From the Algarve’s lively beach towns to the untamed northern coastlines, Portugal’s surf scene thrives on camaraderie and a deep respect for the ocean.

At the heart of this culture is a network of local surf clubs dot Portugal’s coastal towns. These clubs serve as gathering spots where surfers connect, share skills, and participate in local competitions. With surf lessons and rentals available, they welcome both locals and visitors, making it easy for anyone to join Portugal’s thriving surf scene.
Portugal’s appeal as a surf destination also attracts international influences, drawing surfers from across Europe and beyond to its mild climate and diverse waves.

This global connection enriches Portugal’s surf culture, blending styles from California’s relaxed approach to Australia’s high-performance techniques. As a result, Portugal’s surf culture is a melting pot of global surf traditions, providing an open invitation to experience the sport and spirit of surfing in a truly unique way.
What’s Surfing in Portugal Like Today?
As we’ve seen throughout this blog post, Portugal is a surfer’s paradise, boasting some of the
best surf spots
in Europe, each packed with character, charm, and adventure.

Surfing Portugal offers an impressive variety of surf breaks, including everything from challenging point breaks to the more forgiving beach break, each ready to challenge and reward surfers with everything from beginner-friendly waves to the biggest swells for advanced surfers.
With that said, let’s take a look at some of the best
surf spots in Portugal
At the top of the list is Nazaré, renowned for producing the
biggest waves in the world
. Situated on the west coast of Portugal, this Portuguese town is famous for its monster waves in the winter season.

These heaviest waves attract experienced surfers from around the globe. Among them is renowned big-wave surfer Andrew Cotton, who, along with other top athletes, pushes the boundaries of skill and courage to ride waves that reach over 100 feet. It’s a true bucket list destination, drawing thrill-seekers eager to face some of the most extreme conditions in surfing.

Another standout is Peniche, located just south of Nazaré along the same coast. Known for its consistent surf, Peniche offers good waves year-round, making it ideal for surfers of all levels. This larger town is famous for its reliable, world-class breaks, particularly at Praia do Norte, which lies at Peniche’s north end. With plenty of surf schools and rentals, Peniche has become a go-to destination for both beginners and pros looking to book a surf trip.

For those exploring Portugal’s north coast, Santa Cruz provides a mix of powerful beach breaks and hidden gems that bring in big swells from the Atlantic. Santa Cruz is especially inviting during the winter season when the waves are at their best.
Pro Tip
: due to the rugged nature of this coast, be cautious of hazards like sea urchins around certain rocky spots.

Costa da Caparica, a favorite closer to Lisbon, offers year-round waves and easy access to both beach and urban amenities. With two beaches and several breaks within easy reach, Costa da Caparica caters to surfers looking for mellow rides and dependable surf near a lively city atmosphere.

No matter the location, Portugal’s coastlines offer a mix of breaks for all skill levels, from beginner-friendly waters to challenging spots where only experienced surfers dare to venture; you’re bound to find somewhere that tickles your fancy!
Surfing in Portugal: FAQs
Which surf spot in Portugal is the most historical?

It’s tricky to pick just one surf spot, so we’ll quickly list the top ones. Portugal’s most historically significant surf spots include Carcavelos, where surfing began in the 1960s; Ericeira, the only World Surfing Reserve in Europe; Nazaré, now iconic for its record-breaking waves; and Peniche’s Supertubos, known for hosting major competitions. Each spot has played a vital role in shaping Portugal’s rich surf heritage.
What’s the best beach break in Portugal?

One of the best beach breaks in Portugal for today’s surfers is Supertubos in Peniche. Known internationally for its consistent, powerful, and hollow waves, Supertubos creates fast, barreling waves that are ideal for intermediate to advanced surfers. It’s particularly famous for producing “super tubes,” which is where it gets its name. This beach break works best with a southwest swell and offshore northeast wind, making it a high-performance destination and even host to the annual World Surf League (WSL) event.
Which surf spots in Portugal are best for beginners?

Costa da Caparica near Lisbon and Praia do Amado in the Algarve offers gentle waves and safe conditions that are perfect for beginners. These areas provide easy access to surf schools and rentals, making them ideal locations for
learning to surf
in Portugal while still enjoying a mix of beach breaks and great spots to practice.
When is the best time to surf in Portugal?

The summer months, from June to September, provide smaller waves and warmer waters, making them perfect for beginners and casual surfers. However, for those seeking bigger swells, winter offers the best waves along Portugal’s west coast, especially at spots like Nazaré, which is known for its famous big waves.
Do I need a roof rack for my surf trip in Portugal?

If you plan to explore multiple surf spots along the coast, having a roof rack for your vehicle is helpful. Portugal’s coastline is dotted with numerous surf Praia (beach) destinations, and a roof rack makes it easier to transport your boards from spot to spot without hassle.
Who are the best big wave surfers to make history?

Nic von Rupp, known for his fearless approach at Nazaré and for riding massive waves worldwide, and João de Macedo, a prominent figure on the big-wave circuit with impressive performances at locations like Mavericks and Nazaré. António Silva is another standout, regularly tackling Nazaré’s monster waves with skill and tenacity, while Alex Botelho has earned international recognition for his big-wave achievements and resilience after a high-profile injury at Nazaré.
Want to learn more about surfing in Portugal?

7. You

With help from our instructors, you’ll go from fledgling beginner to self-assured intermediate in no time at all. As a result, you’ll be able to take your boards and newfound confidence to other exciting surf destinations around the world, such as

and

MORE INFO
FAQs
What is the best time to visit Lisbon?

The best time to visit Lisbon is during the spring (April to June) and fall (September to October) when the weather is pleasant, and the city is less crowded. Summer (July to August) is popular but can be hot, while winter (December to February) is milder and less busy.
What are the must-visit attractions in Lisbon?

Explore iconic sites like the historic Belém Tower, the Jerónimos Monastery, and São Jorge Castle. Don’t miss the vibrant neighborhoods of Alfama and Bairro Alto, as well as the historic Belem district.
Are there any day trips from Lisbon worth considering?

Yes, take a day trip to Sintra to explore its fairytale-like castles and gardens. Cascais, known for its beautiful beaches and charming streets, and the historic town of Évora are also popular day trip destinations.
What is the best way to get around Lisbon?

Lisbon has an efficient public transportation system, including buses, trams, and the metro. Taxis and ride-sharing services are readily available. Many areas, especially in the city center, are also easily walkable.
What local dishes should I try in Lisbon?

Indulge in local specialties like Pastel de Nata (custard tart), Bacalhau à Brás (shredded codfish), and Bifana (pork sandwich). Explore local markets such as Mercado da Ribeira for a diverse culinary experience.
Is it necessary to speak Portuguese to get around in Lisbon?

While Portuguese is the official language, many people in Lisbon, especially in tourist areas, speak English. Learning a few basic Portuguese phrases is always appreciated, but language should not be a significant barrier.
What are the best neighbourhoods for shopping in Lisbon?

Head to Baixa for mainstream shopping, explore the trendy boutiques in Chiado, and visit the LX Factory for a unique blend of creativity and commerce. The historic Alfama district also offers charming local shops.
Are there any viewpoints with panoramic views of Lisbon?
Yes, visit the Miradouro da Senhora do Monte for stunning city views. São Pedro de Alcântara and Miradouro da Graça are also popular viewpoints offering panoramic perspectives of Lisbon.
What is the nightlife like in Lisbon?

Lisbon has a vibrant nightlife, especially in the Bairro Alto district. Enjoy a variety of bars, fado houses, and clubs. The city comes alive in the evening, offering a diverse range of entertainment for different tastes.
Looking for
Europe’s best waves

? In our guide to surfing in camps Portugal we’ve got the low-down on where and when you can find them. Not including its islands, Portugal boasts 943 kilometres (586 miles) of coastline, which is dotted with countless incredible surf breaks.

The most popular surf spots in Portugal are located in 3 main places: Ericeira, Peniche and the Lisbon coast. Some of the smaller yet amazing spots include Algarve, Alentejo and Porto, which are also home to some serious waves, as are the Azores islands and the Madeira archipelago. The entire continental coast of Portugal is well respected for its year-round consistency and will not disappoint even the most discerning and jaded of surfers.
Brief History of Surfing in Portugal

Portugal’s first national surfing competition was held at Ribeira d’Ilhas in Ericeira in 1977. While that might sound very recent, consider that we are talking about European surfing and specifically a country that was under a somewhat isolated dictatorship until 1974. Portugal really began to put itself firmly on the international surfing map during the 1990s when
the sport exploded in the country

body.alt: Ericeira Surfers

children.text: Ericeira Surfers
Seasons for Surfing in Portugal

You’ll see people surfing Portugal year round, even during the more crowded summer months when it seems like entire country, along with many foreign visitors, comes to fill the its many beautiful beaches. However, the waves are better outside of summer even if the weather can get a bit too cool for casual swimmers and sunbathers.
Which is the best season for surfing in Portugal for beginners?

For beginner surfers the best time of the year to go is summer. This is when the swells are less frequent and the waves are smaller. Summer waves average around 1 – 1.5 meters (3 – 5 feet) with notable exceptions like
Nazaré’s
Praia do Norte, site of many
big wave surfing world records
. In June –
August the most popular
surf schools might get very crowded, especially on the central coast and on the beaches near the capital.
What’s the best time for surfing in Portugal for experts?

For experienced surfers the ideal surf season is late winter to early spring, especially between September and April. During these months swells are definitely more powerful, producing giant waves measuring around 2– 4.5 meters (6 – 15 feet) that are better left to advanced surfers.
Where should you go surfing in Portugal?

The Portuguese coast can be divided into 7 surfing regions. Each of these regions have a number surf camps which offer all kinds of accommodation facilities including private rooms. Most of them offer at breakfast buffet along with other meals, depending on the facility. A lot these surfing camps in Portugal offer a holistic surf and yoga experience.
1. Ericeira
Simply put, surfing Portugal =
surfing Ericeira
. This is Portugal’s — and arguably Europe’s — surfing capital, so anyone on a surf trip in the country would be crazy to skip Ericeira!

At 8 kilometres (5 miles), the smallest surfing region in Portugal is Ericeira, but it is also the one with the highest number of world-class surf spots. Ericeira boasts 9 spots that are famous throughout the global surfing community plus several other notable breaks.
Besides being Europe’s only dedicated
World Surfing Reserve
, Ericeira also hosts the
ASP World Tour

Surf Championship at the legendary Ribeira d’Ilhas beach, known for its near perfect barrels. Experienced, expert and pro-level surfers will also fall in love with Coxos, Cave and São Lourenço, while beginners and the less experienced can learn to surf and sharpen their skills at Foz do Lizandro, São Julião and Praia do Sul.
You can stay at Rapture Camp properties in Ericeira to enjoy an unmatched surfing experience complete with Yoga sessions.
2. Costa Verde or “Green Coast”

This is Portugal’s northern coast, associated with the country’s second largest city, Porto (Oporto). It is immediately north of the Costa de Prata and reaches all the way to the Spanish region of Galicia. Surfing around Porto may take place in colder waters and it has a variety of surf spots available. Notable spots include Aguçadoura, Cabeldo Beach near Viana do Castelo, Azurara near Vila do Conde, Espinho, and Matosinhos (accessible via Porto’s metro system).
3. The Costa de Prata or “Silver Coast”

This extensive stretch of coastline begins north of Ericeira and continues all the way up to the south of Porto at Esmoriz. Notable surf spots in this central coastal region of Portugal include big wave capital Praia do Norte in Nazaré; the legendary Supertubos in Peniche (home of the Moche Rip Curl Pro Portugal); Cortegaça, with its consistent surf and night surfing festival; and the numerous spots around Figueira do Foz and Aveiro.
Surf Portugal With Rapture Surfcamps
4. Lisbon Coast

Only around 50 kilometres (30 miles) south of Ericeira’s incredible surf spots is the Portuguese capital, one of Europe’s thriving cultural centres. Lisbon itself is not to miss, while the Lisbon coast is well worth a visit for its excellent surfing, especially if you’re already in Ericeira. The Lisbon area offers excellent surf all year long. Highlights include great breaks at Estoril, Carcavelos, Praia do Guincho, Praia Grande in Sintra and Praia das Maçás. For smaller crowds and nice beach breaks, head over the Tagus River to the district of Setúbal to surf the Lisbon area’s west coast, particularly the Costa da Caparica.

body.alt: surfing portugal

children.text: Tough competition at Guincho, Lisbon Coast. Pic: Gustavo Veríssimo (Flickr CC)
5. Alentejo Coast
Much of Portugal’s coastline is reminiscent of the California coast. But none more so than the
Alentejo region

, which can resemble the quieter spots of the SoCal surf scene. South of the Lisbon region and north of the Algarve tourism hub. Alentejo is well known for its excellent wines, delicious cuisine and idyllic natural features. There are many great spots along the Vicentine Coast Natural Park, which is located in both Alentejo and the Algarve. Highlights in the Alentejo section include Odeceixe, Zambujeira do Mar, São Torpes, Arrifana and Vila Nova de Milfontes.
6. Algarve Coast

Famous as a tourist paradise for Northern Europeans as well as for portuguese, the southernmost region of Algarve may not be as famous for surfing as, for example, Ericeira. However, there is some very good surfing to be had! If you like your sun brighter and your water a bit warmer, Algarve is worth a shot. Especially on the west coast, which gets north-westerly swells. The south coast can also get nice waves in the winter. Which is the best time to avoid the huge summer crowds anyway. Choose from over 50 surf spots in this unique and beautiful holiday mecca. For beach breaks and medium southerly swells, try Sagres, which is also home to the most south-westerly point in continental Europe; and Lagos, a nice-sized city with plenty to do besides surf. The Algarve’s west coast waves include nice beach towns such as Arifana, Tavira, Monte Clergio and Carrapateira.

body.alt: surfing portugal

children.text: Surfing the Algarve. Pic: Tiago J. G. Fernandes (Flickr CC)
7. The Islands: Azores and Madeira
Though both figuratively and literally off the beaten path, the Azores islands and Madeira archipelago offer unique experiences. Also for travel destinations besides places to surf.

Around 1,360 kilometres (850 miles) west of continental Portugal, the Autonomous Region of the Azores is made up of 9 islands. Each island with its own character. The island of Terceira boasts a cluster of surf spots on its east. The northeast coasts (São Miguel) features what are the archipelago’s most well-known breaks: powerful and consistent Ribeira Grande and Rabo de Peixe. They are both on the island’s north coast. Crowds are small and the surfing can get big and dangerous, especially in the winter, so take care!

body.alt: surfing portugal

children.text: Madeira. Pic: Stefanie Kurtz (Flickr CC)

Situated in the North Atlantic Ocean some 950 kilometres (600 miles) from mainland Portugal, there is Madeira. An Autonomous Region that contains two populated islands: the main island of Madeira and Porto Santo. Most surfing takes place on the main island, with the most famous spot being the barrelling Paul do Mar.

This island has played host to the ISA World Championships and big wave surfing competitions. Other spots include the expert-level Jardim do Mar, Lugar de Baixo and Ponta Pequena. For beginners and intermediates there is the calmer São Vicente, located on the north coast.
In addition to the exceptional surf, many of these regions offer surf camps that provide holistic experiences, including yoga sessions and accommodation facilities.

Whether you’re a seasoned pro or a beginner looking to catch your first wave, Portugal’s diverse coastline and consistent surf will not disappoint. So, grab your board, head to the waves, and experience the thrill of surfing in this European surfers’ paradise.
