2. Hike and Volcano Board:

For thrill-seekers, Nicaragua offers the unique opportunity to hike up an active volcano and then sled down on a board. Cerro Negro near León is the most popular destination for volcano boarding, providing an adrenaline-pumping experience like no other.
3. Explore Miraflores National Park:

Located in Estelí, Miraflores National Park is a haven for nature enthusiasts. The park is known for its stunning cloud forest, picturesque waterfalls, and diverse wildlife. Immerse yourself in treks through lush landscapes and immerse yourself in Nicaragua’s natural beauty.
4. Fish in the Freshwater Lakes:

Nicaragua is home to numerous freshwater lakes, providing excellent fishing opportunities. Cast your line in Lake Nicaragua or Lake Managua and try your luck at catching tarpon, snook, and rainbow bass. Fishing charters are available for both beginners and experienced anglers.
5. Experience San Juan del Sur:

As the most popular tourist town in Nicaragua, San Juan del Sur offers a vibrant blend of surf, sun, beaches, and nightlife. Relax on the sandy shores, take surf lessons, indulge in fresh seafood, and immerse yourself in the lively atmosphere of this coastal gem.
Nicaragua offers a diverse range of experiences for travellers seeking adventure, relaxation, and natural beauty. Whether you’re into
surfing

, hiking, volcano boarding, fishing, or simply exploring stunning national parks, this Central American gem has something for everyone. Don’t miss the chance to visit Miraflores National Park in Estelí or soak up the sun in San Juan del Sur, making your trip to Nicaragua truly unforgettable.
Day Trips from Nicaragua to Costa Rica

Are you planning a trip to Nicaragua and looking for exciting day trips to nearby Costa Rica? Look no further! Here are some amazing day trip options that will allow you to explore the beauty and diversity of Costa Rica while based in Nicaragua. Remember to follow recommendations from Nicaraguan authorities when planning your day trips to ensure a smooth and safe experience.
1. Arenal Volcano National Park:

Just a short drive from the Nicaraguan border, Arenal Volcano National Park is a must-visit destination. Witness the majestic Arenal Volcano, hike through lush rainforests, and relax in natural hot springs. This day trip will leave you in awe of Costa Rica’s stunning landscapes.
2. Monteverde Cloud Forest Biological Reserve:

Embark on an adventure to the famous Monteverde Cloud Forest Biological Reserve. This unique ecosystem is home to a wide variety of flora and fauna, including rare orchids and the elusive quetzal bird. Explore the treetop walkways, take a thrilling zipline tour, or go on a guided nature hike.
3. Manuel Antonio National Park:

Experience the best of Costa Rica’s wildlife at Manuel Antonio National Park. This coastal gem boasts pristine beaches, lush rainforests, and abundant wildlife. Take a hike through the park’s trails, spot monkeys swinging from trees, and relax on the picturesque beaches. Don’t forget your camera!
4. Tortuguero National Park:

If you’re a nature lover, a day trip to Tortuguero National Park is a must. Known as Costa Rica’s “Amazon of the Caribbean,” this park is a haven for wildlife enthusiasts. Take a boat tour through the park’s intricate canal system and spot monkeys, sloths, toucans, and even sea turtles during nesting season.
5. Rincon de la Vieja National Park:

For those seeking adventure, a day trip to Rincon de la Vieja National Park is perfect. Hike to the summit of the active volcano, explore bubbling mud pots and hot springs, and cool off under beautiful waterfalls. This park offers a unique combination of volcanic activity and natural beauty.
6. Palo Verde National Park:

Discover the wonders of Palo Verde National Park, a wetland sanctuary teeming with wildlife. Join a boat tour along the Tempisque River and spot crocodiles, monkeys, iguanas, and a wide variety of bird species. Immerse yourself in the tranquility of this biodiverse paradise.
7. La Fortuna Waterfall:

If you’re looking for a refreshing escape, visit the La Fortuna Waterfall. Located near the town of La Fortuna, this breathtaking waterfall cascades into a picturesque pool. Take a dip in the cool water, hike through the surrounding rainforest, and enjoy the natural beauty of this hidden gem.
Tamarindo Beach

Spend a day relaxing on the golden sands of Tamarindo Beach. This popular beach destination offers excellent surfing opportunities, vibrant nightlife, and a wide range of restaurants and shops. Whether you’re a beach lover or a thrill-seeker, Tamarindo has something for everyone.

Embark on these incredible day trips from Nicaragua to Costa Rica and create unforgettable memories. From volcanoes and rainforests to beaches and wildlife, Costa Rica has it all. Don’t miss out on the chance to explore this neighboring paradise during your trip to Nicaragua.
10 Things to Know About Nicaragua Before You Plan a Trip There – Summarised
1. Safety:

Nicaragua is generally considered safe for tourists, but it’s always important to take precautions like avoiding isolated areas at night and keeping an eye on your belongings. Stay informed about the current situation and follow the advice of local authorities.
2. Language:

The official language in Nicaragua is Spanish. While English might be spoken in tourist areas, it’s helpful to learn a few basic Spanish phrases to navigate the country more easily and communicate with locals.
3. Currency:

The currency in Nicaragua is the Nicaraguan Cordoba (NIO). It’s recommended to exchange some currency upon arrival or withdraw cash from ATMs. Credit cards are widely accepted in major establishments, but it’s always good to have some cash on hand for smaller businesses and street vendors.
4. Climate:

Nicaragua has a tropical climate with two main seasons: dry season (November to April) and rainy season (May to October). Be prepared for high humidity and occasional rain showers, especially during the rainy season. Pack accordingly and plan outdoor activities accordingly.
5. Natural Beauty:

Nicaragua is renowned for its stunning landscapes, including volcanoes, lakes, rainforests, and beautiful beaches. From hiking up volcanoes to exploring colonial towns, there is no shortage of natural beauty and activities to enjoy.
6. Cultural Heritage:

Nicaragua has a rich cultural heritage influenced by indigenous traditions, Spanish colonialism, and Afro-Caribbean influences. Explore the colourful markets, indulge in traditional cuisine, and immerse yourself in the vibrant local culture.
7. Health and Safety:

It is advisable to consult a healthcare professional before traveling to Nicaragua to ensure you are up to date on necessary vaccinations. Travel insurance is also recommended to cover any unexpected medical expenses.
8. Transportation:

Nicaragua has a variety of transportation options, including buses, taxis, and rental cars. Public buses are the most affordable, but can be crowded and less comfortable. Taxis are readily available in cities, while rental cars offer more flexibility for exploring the country.
9. Volcano Boarding:
Nicaragua is the only place in the world where you can go volcano boarding. Cerro Negro, an active volcano, offers the unique adventure of sliding down its ash-covered slopes on a board.
10. Ecotourism Opportunities:

Nicaragua is known for its commitment to eco-friendly and sustainable tourism. From wildlife reserves to protected areas, there are plenty of opportunities to experience the country’s diverse flora and fauna while supporting conservation efforts.
Remember to research and plan your trip well in advance to make the most of your time in Nicaragua. Embrace the beauty, culture, and adventure that this Central American gem has to offer.

In conclusion, Nicaragua is a country filled with rich history, diverse landscapes, and a vibrant culture that is waiting to be explored. From its stunning beaches to its breath-taking volcanoes, this Central American gem offers a little something for everyone.

We have delved into the country’s fascinating history, highlighting its tumultuous past and its journey towards peace and stability. We have also explored Nicaragua’s natural wonders, from the lush rainforests and unique wildlife of its national parks to the pristine beaches that adorn its coastline.

But Nicaragua is more than just its natural beauty. Its warm and welcoming people, delicious cuisine, and vibrant traditions make it a truly unforgettable destination. Whether you’re seeking adventure, relaxation, or a deeper understanding of a country’s cultural heritage, Nicaragua has it all.

As you plan your trip to Nicaragua, remember to immerse yourself in the local customs and traditions, try the mouth-watering traditional dishes, and take the time to connect with the friendly locals. And don’t forget to explore the hidden gems that lie off the beaten path – you never know what hidden treasures you might discover.

So, whether you’re an adrenaline junkie looking for an unforgettable surf session, a nature lover yearning to explore untouched landscapes, or a culture enthusiast eager to delve into Nicaragua’s rich heritage, this country has something to offer you.

Nicaragua is a destination that will captivate your heart and leave you with lasting memories. So, pack your bags, embrace the unknown, and get ready for an adventure of a lifetime in this Central American paradise. Nicaragua is waiting for you, ready to share its beauty and stories with you.

FAQs
What is the best time to visit Nicaragua?
The dry season, from November to April, is considered the best time to visit Nicaragua. During this period, you can enjoy sunny weather and minimal rainfall.
How can I get to Nicaragua?
Nicaragua is accessible by air, with international airports in Managua, the capital. Major airlines offer flights to Nicaragua, and domestic transportation includes buses and taxis.
What are the visa requirements for visiting Nicaragua?

Many countries, including the United States and most European nations, do not require a visa for stays up to 90 days. However, it’s essential to check specific visa requirements based on your nationality.
Are vaccinations required for travel to Nicaragua?
While no specific vaccinations are required for entry, it’s advisable to be up-to-date on routine vaccinations. Consult with a healthcare professional for recommendations based on your travel plans.
What is the local currency in Nicaragua?
The official currency is the Nicaraguan Córdoba (NIO). U.S. dollars are widely accepted in tourist areas, but it’s recommended to have local currency for smaller transactions.
What types of accommodations are available in Nicaragua?
Nicaragua offers a range of accommodations, from luxury resorts to budget-friendly hostels. Options include beachfront hotels, eco-lodges, and colonial-style guesthouses.
Is it safe to travel in Nicaragua?
Nicaragua is generally considered safe for tourists. However, like any destination, it’s essential to stay aware of your surroundings, follow local guidelines, and take standard safety precautions.
What are the must-visit places in Nicaragua?
Explore the colonial charm of Granada, the volcanic landscapes of Ometepe Island, the historic city of Leon, and the surf-friendly beaches of San Juan del Sur.
What outdoor activities are popular in Nicaragua?

Nicaragua offers diverse outdoor activities, including surfing, volcano trekking, zip-lining, and exploring lush rainforests. The country’s natural beauty provides ample opportunities for adventure enthusiasts.
Are there surfing opportunities in Nicaragua?
Yes, Nicaragua is a fantastic destination for surfers. Places like San Juan del Sur offer great waves for all skill levels. Consider surf camps or lessons for a memorable surfing experience.
Are there surf schools or lessons available in Nicaragua?

Yes, Nicaragua offers various surf schools and camps, particularly in popular surfing towns like San Juan del Sur. These schools cater to all skill levels, from beginners looking to catch their first wave to advanced surfers seeking new challenges.
What should I know about healthcare services in Nicaragua?

Healthcare services in Nicaragua can vary significantly, especially outside major cities. In rural areas, the availability and quality of medical facilities are limited. In cases of serious illness or injury, medical evacuation might be necessary due to insufficient emergency medical services and the lack of adequate medical professionals. It is advisable to have travel insurance that covers medical evacuation.

Getting to Portugal by road

Getting to Portugal by road offers the flexibility and freedom of a road trip, allowing travelers to explore neighboring countries and enjoy scenic routes. Portugal shares its borders with Spain, but you can also plan to get here from Spain’s neighboring countries, such as France and even Morocco (by ferry); there are several options for reaching Portugal.

Border crossings between Portugal and these countries are well-developed, making the journey seamless. However, it is essential to note that certain documentation, such as a valid passport or visa, may be required depending on your nationality.

Portugal has an extensive network of toll roads, known as “Autoestradas,” which are well-maintained and provide efficient connectivity to various regions within the country. It is essential to have the appropriate documentation and permits for using toll roads, such as an electronic toll device or a prepaid card.

For tourists traveling by car, it is crucial to familiarize themselves with Portugal’s driving rules and regulations, including speed limits and parking restrictions. Whether embarking on a road trip from a neighboring country or exploring the diverse regions within Portugal, traveling by road offers a unique and immersive experience, allowing visitors to discover the country’s hidden gems at their own pace.
Getting to Portugal by sea
Getting to Portugal by sea is a popular and convenient option for travelers. There are several options available, including ferry services and cruises.

Ferry services provide a convenient way to reach Portugal from neighboring countries such as Spain and Morocco. The ports of entry for these ferry services include Faro, Portimão, Lisbon, Porto, and Viana do Castelo. Multiple routes are available, allowing you to choose the most convenient and cost-effective option for your trip. However, specific visa requirements may depend on your passport, so enquire about these details beforehand.

Cruise ships also offer a luxurious and leisurely way to reach Portugal. Many major cruise lines include Portugal in their itineraries, making it easy to explore the country while enjoying the amenities and entertainment on board. When planning your trip to Portugal by sea, consider the options available and choose the one that best suits your preferences and schedule. Whether you opt for a ferry service or a cruise, arriving in Portugal by sea offers a unique and memorable travel experience. Please do your homework regarding the paperwork before paying for any service.
How do you get around Portugal using public transportation?

Portugal has a robust network of public transport buses and trains covering the entire country. Public transportation in Portugal is cheap, convenient, and easy to use. Besides buses and trains, you can also use the various bike and car rentals throughout the country.

Bus services in Portugal are categorized into three main types: Carreiras, Expressos and Rápidas, and Alta Qualidade. Carreiras are local bus services that connect smaller towns and villages not served by the rail network, offering an economical option for short-distance travel. Expressos and Rápidas are faster services connecting major cities and towns, balancing speed and cost. Alta Qualidade services provide the highest level of comfort and speed, often with amenities such as Wi-Fi and more spacious seating, but at a higher price. Online ticket sales are available for these services. However, travelers may need to navigate various private operators and potential scheduling limitations.
Getting around Portugal by high-speed train
Getting around Portugal
by train is an affordable and convenient way to explore the country. The train network system in Portugal is well-developed, with both regular and high-speed trains available.

Regular trains are popular for traveling between major cities and day trips to nearby destinations. They offer comfortable seating and beautiful views of the Portuguese countryside. The ticket prices for regular trains are relatively low, making it a budget-friendly option for travelers.

High-speed trains are also available for those looking for a faster mode of transportation. These trains connect major cities like Lisbon, Porto, and Faro, allowing passengers to reach their destinations quickly and comfortably. Opting for a high-speed train offers advantages over flying, such as cost-effectiveness and reduced travel time, particularly for visitors planning an itinerary that focuses on Lisbon and Porto. Although the ticket prices for high-speed trains are slightly higher than regular trains, the time saved is well worth it.

Some popular train routes in Portugal include Lisbon to Porto, which offers stunning coastal views, and Lisbon to Faro, the gateway to beautiful beaches in the Algarve region. Additionally, day trips to places like Sintra, Cascais, and Coimbra are easily accessible by train. To plan your train journey, it’s recommended to visit the
official train websites

to check train times and purchase tickets. The extensive regional train network allows you to easily navigate Portugal and explore its beautiful cities, beaches, and countryside. So hop on a train and enjoy the convenience and affordability of
traveling around Portugal
by rail.
Getting around Portugal by buses

Another option when it comes to getting around Portugal bus travel is to explore different cities and regions. The country has an extensive bus route network connecting major cities and towns, making it easy to navigate and explore.

One of the advantages of traveling by bus in Portugal is its affordability. Bus tickets are often cheaper than other transport modes, such as trains or flights. This makes it an ideal choice for budget travelers or those looking to save money while still enjoying their trip.

Additionally, buses offer a high level of convenience, as they provide direct connections to popular destinations. For example, if you’re planning to travel from Lisbon to Lagos or Braga to Faro, there are several bus routes available that can take you there without any hassle. Local services are available to connect smaller towns and villages not served by the national rail network.

The approximate costs of these bus journeys vary depending on the distance and the bus company you choose. On average, a bus ticket from Lisbon to Lagos can cost around €20-€25, while a trip from Braga to Faro may range from €15-€20. It’s important to note that prices may vary during peak travel seasons.

Overall, bus travel in Portugal is a great way to explore the country while staying within budget. Its affordable fares and convenient routes offer a reliable and efficient mode of transportation for locals and tourists alike. So sit back, relax, and enjoy the scenic views as you travel across this beautiful country by bus.
Getting around Portugal by car/bike rentals

When planning a trip to Portugal, renting a car or bike is one of the most convenient ways to get around the country. Renting a car gives you the freedom to explore at your own pace and venture into the off-beaten path. Rental cars are available in major towns and cities, providing convenience for travelers. However, we recommend you do this, keeping safety in mind.
There are several car rental companies in Portugal, and it’s best to book in advance to secure the best rates and availability. Websites like
RentalCars
can help you compare prices and find the perfect car for your trip.

If you prefer a more eco-friendly option or want to experience Portugal from a different perspective, bike rentals are also available in major cities and tourist areas. Riding a bike allows you to immerse yourself in the local culture and enjoy the beautiful scenery.

Consider flying into Porto and out of Faro to make your trip more convenient. This way, you can explore the northern part of the country, including the vibrant city of Porto with its delicious food scene and iconic landmarks, such as the Ribeira district and
Dom Luís I Bridge
. From Porto, you can also take a day trip to the stunning Douro Valley and enjoy wine tastings and breathtaking views of the vineyards.

In Faro, you can relax on the beautiful beaches of the Algarve region or explore the charming old town with its narrow streets and historic buildings. Renting a car or bike in both cities will allow you to make the most of your time and experience Portugal’s diverse attractions and landscapes.
If you plan to get a bike or car rental on your next trip to Portugal, ensure you have a valid license and all other necessary documents.
Best Time to Visit Portugal
Portugal can be enjoyed throughout all seasons, so the best time to visit Portugal will depend entirely on your preferences and the purpose of your visit.
The seasons of spring, which lasts from March to May, and autumn, which lasts from September to October, are highly recommended for visiting Portugal. You will find
fewer crowds
and enjoy mild temperatures during this time.

Witness the blooming landscapes and the re-opening of the many attractions in Portugal during Spring as the quieter cold months end. During autumn, you can explore Portugal’s countryside and the plethora of winery activities Portugal has to offer.
However, if you don’t mind the cold months, you can visit Portugal in December and January and enjoy the festive festivities of the season.
If you are visiting
Portugal to surf
along the
many surf spots
it has to offer, the ideal time to visit would be between
May and September
for
beginner surfers
and between September and April for experienced surfers.
No matter when you choose to visit Portugal,
Portugal has a lot of things to offer all year round

. From the stunning beaches along the coast to the charming cities like Lisbon and Porto, there is plenty to see and do. So, plan your trip accordingly and make the most of your time in this beautiful country.
Weather and Climate

Portugal boasts a Mediterranean climate characterized by mild winters and warm summers, making it a year-round destination. However, the weather can vary significantly depending on the region. The north tends to be cooler and wetter, while the south enjoys more sunshine and higher temperatures.

The best times to visit Portugal are spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the temperatures are pleasant, ranging from 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F), and the tourist crowds are thinner, allowing for a more relaxed experience. Spring brings blooming landscapes and vibrant festivals, while autumn offers a chance to explore the countryside and participate in winery activities.

Summer (June to August) can be pretty hot, especially in the southern regions like the Algarve, with temperatures often exceeding 30°C (86°F). This is the perfect time for beach lovers to soak up the sun and enjoy the coastal beauty. However, be prepared for larger crowds and higher prices during this peak tourist season.

Winter (December to February) is generally mild, with temperatures ranging from 10°C to 15°C (50°F to 59°F). While it may be cooler, it’s still a great time to explore Portugal’s cities and enjoy the festive atmosphere. Pack some layers for the cooler mornings and evenings, and don’t forget your umbrella, as winter can bring rain.

No matter when you visit, Portugal’s weather can be unpredictable, so it’s wise to pack layers and be prepared for sudden changes in temperature or precipitation. Sunscreen and a hat are essential for sunny days, while a light jacket will keep you comfortable during cooler evenings.
Food in Portugal

Get ready for a delightful journey that Portuguese cuisine will take you on when you are here. Portuguese cuisine leans more towards seafood and meat-based dishes, showcasing the richness of their local produce and traditional cooking techniques. This means that vegetarian and vegan travelers may sometimes need help finding something that suits their dietary preferences. However, having said that finding
good plant-based meals in Portugal
is not impossible as the options are growing.

One must-try treat in Portugal is the iconic Pastel de Nata, a delicious custard tart with a crispy puff pastry shell. These delectable pastries often enjoyed with a sprinkle of cinnamon and powdered sugar, can be found in bakeries and cafes across the country.

Another popular dish to savor is Migas, a comforting blend of bread, garlic, olive oil, and regional spices. This traditional dish varies from region to region, offering a unique taste experience wherever you go.

To add a bit of sweetness and kick to your culinary exploration, make sure to try Ginja liqueur. This cherry liqueur is typically served in small chocolate cups and is commonly enjoyed as an aperitif or digestive. Whether you indulge in the meat and fish-based dishes that Portugal is renowned for or opt for the vegetarian-friendly options available, exploring the country’s rich and diverse culinary traditions is a journey worth savoring.
Accommodation in Portugal
Since Portugal is a hot tourist destination, you will find a variety of options. From luxury hotels to cozy guesthouses to
surf
camps in Portugal, there is something to suit every traveler’s needs.

Tourists can easily find hotels as they are widely available throughout the country in a diverse range of cost brackets – from budget-friendly options to high-end rooms. Hotels offer convenience and amenities, especially in major cities and popular tourist areas. Private rentals like apartments and villas provide a more intimate and home-like experience.
There are various booking platforms that can be used to find the perfect place to stay in Portugal.
Booking.com

is one of the most popular websites, offering a wide range of options and competitive prices. Platforms like Airbnb and HomeAway also provide private rentals for those seeking a more unique and personalized experience.

If you are visiting during the cold months, we recommend that you enquire if your accommodation has heating options as the temperature can fall quite a bit during the nights in cold months. Irrespective of that, conduct thorough research into the places you are booking to ensure that all the amenities you seek are available.
Currency in Portugal

The official currency accepted throughout Portugal is the Euro, from the bustling city centers to the countryside. If you need to exchange your currency for Euro, you can easily find currency exchange offices in major cities and tourist areas. Just ask, “Where is the nearest currency exchange office?” and the locals will be happy to help. Credit cards are widely accepted in most establishments, so you can conveniently pay with your card.

When planning your trip to Portugal, knowing some basic phrases related to currency exchange is always helpful. Here are a few examples: “Onde fica o câmbio mais próximo?” (Where is the nearest currency exchange?) and “Posso pagar com cartão de crédito?” (Can I pay with a credit card?). With the Euro as Portugal’s currency, you can enjoy a hassle-free trip, knowing you’ll have no trouble using your money throughout the country.
Top 5 Cities to Visit in Portugal
We are not saying that there are only 5 cities that are worth visiting when traveling to Portugal, but if you are short on time, here are the 5 cities you should not miss:
1. Porto
Situated along the Douro River, Porto is the second-largest city in Portugal. It is known for its charming and colorful houses, medieval streets, and exquisite port wine.
Vila Nova

de Gaia, located across the Douro River, is known for its port lodges and stunning views of Porto. Visit the Ribeira district, explore the riverside promenade, or take a boat tour to admire the incredible architecture. Take advantage of the iconic
Clerigos Tower
or the beautiful
Livraria Lello bookstore
, which is said to have inspired J.K. Rowling’s Harry Potter series.
2. Sintra
Sintra

is famous for its fairytale-like Pena Palace, an enchanting blend of architectural styles, vibrant colors, and small forests. Other must-see attractions include the Moorish Castle and the mysterious Initiation Well at Quinta da Regaleira. Stroll through the historic center to discover charming cafés and boutique shops.
3. Coimbra

As Portugal’s university city, Coimbra offers a unique blend of youthful energy and rich history. Explore the University of Coimbra, one of the oldest universities in the world, and visit the stunning
Joanina Library

. Take a walk through the picturesque streets of the historic center and marvel at the intricate azulejo tiles. For a tranquil experience, visit the breathtaking Mondego River and enjoy the panoramic views.
4. Faro

Located in the sunny Algarve region, Faro offers beautiful beaches and captivating coastal views. Explore the historic center with its charming cobblestone streets and medieval walls. Visit the beautiful
Faro Cathedral
and enjoy panoramic views from its tower. Take a boat trip to the nearby Ria Formosa Natural Park, a paradise for bird-watching and nature lovers.
5. Évora

A UNESCO World Heritage Site, Évora is a charming and well-preserved medieval city. Stroll through the narrow streets of the historic center and admire the impressive Roman Temple. Visit the bone chapel, known as Capela dos Ossos, which has its macabre but fascinating interior covered in human skulls and bones. Don’t miss the stunning Évora Cathedral or the picturesque Giraldo Square. Each of these cities in Portugal offers unique experiences and attractions, making them must-visit destinations for any traveler.
5 Best Things to Do in Portugal

Portugal is a country rich in history, culture, and natural beauty. From the vibrant city of Lisbon to the stunning coastal areas, there is no shortage of things to do and see in this remarkable destination. Here are some top activities to include in your itinerary:
1. Immerse yourself in the culture of Lisbon

Begin your ultimate trip to Portugal by exploring Lisbon’s vibrant capital city. Lisbon’s Santa Apolonia station is a key arrival point, well-connected to the city’s metro system. Wander through the charming streets of Alfama, the oldest district, and listen to the melancholic melodies of Fado music, a traditional genre deeply rooted in Portuguese culture. Discover iconic landmarks like the Jerónimos Monastery, which showcase the country’s rich history. Don’t miss the chance to indulge in the delectable local cuisine, sampling pastéis de nata (custard tarts) and fresh seafood dishes.
2. Embark on an ‘Age of Discovery’ tour at a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Feel the spirit of Portugal’s maritime past by visiting historic sites connected to the Age of Discovery. Head to Belém, where you can explore the Monument to the Discoveries, a tribute to the country’s famous explorers. Visit the awe-inspiring Belém Tower, a fortress overlooking the Tagus River, and step inside the majestic Mosteiro dos Jerónimos, a symbol of Portugal’s golden era.
3. Explore Sintra, the castle town of Portugal

Just a short train ride from Lisbon lies the enchanting town of Sintra, a UNESCO World Heritage site dotted with fairytale-like castles and palaces. Marvel at the colorful façade of the Pena Palace, perched on a hilltop, and wander through the lush gardens of Quinta da Regaleira. Don’t miss the opportunity to try the local specialty, queijadas de Sintra, a delicious cheesecake enjoyed for centuries.
4. Discover the pristine beaches

Portugal is renowned for its stunning coastline and beautiful beaches. Visit the picturesque town of Cascais, situated on the Atlantic Ocean, and soak up the sun on its sandy shores. Spending a few hours at the beach is a great way to relax and enjoy Portugal’s stunning coastline. For a more adventurous beach experience, head to the Algarve region, known for its hidden beaches and dramatic cliffs. Explore the breathtaking rock formations in Ponta da Piedade and relax on the pristine sand of Praia da Marinha, often regarded as one of the most beautiful beaches in the world.
5. Surf your heart out in Portugal.
Portugal is a surfer’s paradise
with its consistent waves and ideal weather. Whether you’re a beginner or an experienced surfer, plenty of
surf camps
and schools along the coast cater to your needs. From the famous breaks of
Ericeira
to the laid-back vibe of Sagres, you’ll find
surf spots
to suit every level.
Experience the thrill of riding the waves and immerse yourself in Portugal’s vibrant
surf culture

. In a nutshell, Portugal offers a myriad of activities for every traveler. From soaking in the history and culture of Lisbon to exploring fairy tale castles in Sintra, and from basking on stunning beaches to riding the waves along the coast, a trip to Portugal is an incredible adventure filled with unforgettable experiences.

UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Portugal

Portugal is home to 17 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, each a testament to the country’s rich cultural and historical heritage. These sites offer a glimpse into Portugal’s past and are must-visit destinations for any traveler.
The Historic Centre of Porto
: Wander through the medieval streets of Porto, a city known for its charming architecture and vibrant atmosphere. Take advantage of the iconic Ribeira district along the Douro River.
The Tower of Belém in Lisbon
: This stunning fortress overlooking the Tagus River symbolizes Portugal’s Age of Discovery. Its intricate Manueline architecture is a sight to behold.
The Jerónimos Monastery in Lisbon
: Another masterpiece of Manueline architecture, this monastery is a tribute to Portugal’s maritime explorers. The ornate details and grand cloisters are genuinely awe-inspiring.
The Pena Palace in Sintra
: Perched on a hilltop, this colorful palace looks like something out of a fairy tale. Its eclectic mix of architectural styles and lush gardens make it a must-visit.
The Moorish Castle in Sintra
: Explore the ancient ruins of this castle, which offers panoramic views of the surrounding landscape. It’s a great spot for history buffs and nature lovers alike.
The Historic Centre of Évora
: Stroll through the well-preserved medieval streets of Évora, a city rich in history. Highlights include the Roman Temple and the macabre Capela dos Ossos (Chapel of Bones).
The Convent of Christ in Tomar
: This former Templar stronghold is a fascinating blend of Gothic, Manueline, and Renaissance architecture. The intricate details and historical significance make it a must-see.
The Monastery of Batalha
: A masterpiece of Gothic architecture, this monastery was built to commemorate the Battle of Aljubarrota. Its stunning stained glass windows and intricate stonework are breathtaking.
The Prehistoric Rock Art Sites in the Côa Valley
: Discover ancient rock carvings that date back thousands of years. These sites offer a unique glimpse into prehistoric life and art.
The Landscape of the Pico Island Vineyard Culture
: Located in the Azores, this UNESCO site showcases the unique vineyard culture of Pico Island. The volcanic landscape and traditional wine-making methods are fascinating to explore.
The Garrison Border Town of Elvas and its Fortifications
: This well-preserved fortress town offers a glimpse into Portugal’s military history. The star-shaped fortifications and historic buildings are impressive.
The University of Coimbra – Alta and Sofia
: One of the oldest universities in the world, Coimbra’s university is a hub of learning and culture. The stunning Joanina Library is a highlight.
The Royal Building of Mafra – Palace, Basilica, Convent, Cerco Garden, and Hunting Park (Tapada)
: This grand complex is a testament to Portugal’s royal history. The opulent palace and serene gardens are worth exploring.
The Sanctuary of Bom Jesus do Monte in Braga
: This baroque sanctuary is known for its stunning staircase and beautiful views. It’s a place of pilgrimage and a symbol of faith.

These UNESCO World Heritage Sites are a testament to Portugal’s rich cultural and historical heritage. Include them in your itinerary for a deeper understanding of the country’s past and architectural marvels.
Safety and Accessibility
Portugal is generally a safe country to visit, with lower crime rates than other European destinations. However, taking standard precautions is always wise to ensure a safe and enjoyable trip.
Safety Tips for Travelers in Portugal
Be aware of your surroundings
: In crowded areas and tourist hotspots, stay vigilant and monitor your belongings.
Keep valuables secure
: Consider using a money belt or a secure bag to keep your valuables safe.
Avoid walking alone in dimly lit areas at night
: Stick to well-lit and populated areas to ensure your safety.
Use licensed taxis or ride-sharing services
: Always check the driver’s ID before getting in, and avoid unlicensed taxis.
Be mindful of pickpocketing and petty theft
: Keep your belongings close in crowded areas and be cautious of your surroundings.
Avoid displaying signs of wealth
: Refrain from wearing expensive jewelry or watches that might attract unwanted attention.
Keep your hotel room door locked
: Use the hotel safe, if available, to store your valuables.
Be cautious when using ATMs
: Avoid using ATMs in isolated areas, and be aware of your surroundings when withdrawing cash.
Accessible Transportation in Portugal

Portugal has made significant strides in improving accessibility for visitors with disabilities. Many public buildings, including museums, restaurants, and hotels, are equipped with wheelchair ramps and accessible toilets. The public transportation system is also relatively accessible, with many options available for travelers with disabilities.
Buses and Trains

: Many bus companies, including Rede Expressos and Rodonorte, have accessible buses with ramps and wheelchair spaces. The train network also offers accessible services, with staff trained to assist passengers with disabilities.
Metro Systems
: The metro systems in Lisbon and Porto are equipped with elevators and ramps, making it easier for travelers with disabilities to navigate the platforms.
Accessible Taxis
: Many taxi companies offer accessible vehicles, and some cities have dedicated accessible taxi services. It’s advisable to book these services in advance to ensure availability.
MyWay Service

: This personalized assistance service is available for passengers with disabilities on public transportation. It can be booked in advance through the transportation company’s website or by contacting them directly.

While Portugal is relatively accessible, it’s always a good idea to research and plan ahead to ensure you can access the places you want to visit. With the right preparation, travelers with disabilities can enjoy a comfortable and enjoyable trip to Portugal.

By following these safety tips and taking advantage of the accessible transportation options, you can have a safe and enjoyable trip to Portugal, exploring all the beauty and culture this country has to offer.
10 Things to Know for Your Trip to Portugal (summarized)
Planning a trip to Portugal? Here are the top 10 things you should know and not miss on your trip to Portugal:
1. Travel Insurance:
Purchasing travel insurance before your trip is crucial. This ensures you are covered for any unexpected events or emergencies.
2. Lisbon:
Visit Portugal’s capital city, Lisbon
. Explore the charming neighborhoods, indulge in delicious cuisine, and enjoy the vibrant nightlife.
3. Sintra:
Don’t miss the enchanting town of Sintra, known for its fairytale-like palaces, such as the famous Pena Palace. Take a day trip from Lisbon to experience its magical atmosphere.
4. Benagil Caves:
Discover the stunning Benagil caves in the Algarve region. These natural wonders can only be accessed by boat and offer breathtaking views.
5. Costa Nova Beach:
Head to Costa Nova Beach near Aveiro to admire its colorful striped houses and relax on its pristine sandy shores.
6. Porto:
Explore the vibrant city of Porto, renowned for its port wine production. Visit the cellars, stroll along the Douro River, and soak up the city’s rich history.
7. Douro Valley:
Take a trip to the picturesque Douro Valley, known for its vineyards and scenic landscapes. Enjoy wine tastings and river cruises as you explore this beautiful region.
8. Porto Wine:
Indulge in the world-famous Porto wine and discover its unique flavors. Take a tour of the cellars and learn about their production process.
9. Fado Music:
Experience the soul-stirring sounds of Fado music, Portugal’s traditional music genre. Enjoy live performances at local bars and restaurants in Lisbon and Porto.
10. Pastel de Nata:

Don’t leave Portugal without trying the delicious Pastel de Nata, a famous Portuguese custard tart. Sample these sweet treats at local bakeries throughout the country. Whether you’re exploring Lisbon’s narrow streets, getting lost in the beauty of Sintra, or savoring the flavors of Porto, Portugal offers a diverse range of experiences that will leave you wanting to return for more.

FAQs
What is the best time to visit Portugal?
Portugal is a year-round destination, but the spring (April to June) and fall (September to October) offer pleasant weather with fewer crowds. Summer (July to August) is ideal for beach lovers.
How can I
get around in Portugal

Portugal has an efficient public transportation system, including trains and buses. Renting a car is a popular option for exploring more remote areas, and major cities also have well-connected metro systems. Nearly all metro stations in Porto are accessible for wheelchair users, with ramps, elevators, and dedicated spaces on trains.
Are there visa requirements for visiting Portugal?

Portugal is part of the Schengen Area, and travelers from many countries, including the US and EU member states, do not need a visa for short stays (up to 90 days). Check visa requirements based on your nationality.
What are the must-visit places in Portugal?
Explore the historic charm of Lisbon, the picturesque Douro Valley, the Algarve’s coastal beauty, and Porto’s cultural richness. Each region offers a unique experience.
Is English widely spoken in Portugal?
While Portuguese is the official language, English is widely spoken, especially in tourist areas. Most locals in the hospitality industry understand and communicate in English.
What outdoor activities are popular in Portugal?
Portugal offers diverse outdoor activities, including
surfing
, hiking, wine tasting in the Douro Valley, and exploring historic sites. The Algarve is known for its stunning beaches and water activities.
Are there surfing opportunities in Portugal?
Yes, Portugal is a renowned surf destination with consistent waves along the coast. Popular surf spots include
Ericeira
, Peniche, and the Algarve. Surf schools and camps cater to all skill levels.
Are surf camps in Portugal suitable for all skill levels?

Yes, surf camps in Portugal cater to surfers of all levels, from beginners to advanced. Experienced instructors provide lessons, and the diverse coastline offers a variety of waves suitable for different skill levels.
What is the best time of year to
book a surf camp in Portugal
The
surf season in Portugal
typically runs from September to April, with the autumn and winter months providing optimal conditions for larger
swells
. However, surf camps operate year-round, offering lessons and experiences tailored to seasonal conditions.

For example, an area containing one incredible surf spot is not necessarily enough to put an entire destination at the top. Yet, towns like Ericeira in
Portugal
are full of
great breaks
and in close proximity of countless others which makes it a great spot for surfing for anyone looking to explore more than one location. Furthermore, a list may include
Bali
, an undisputed
surfing paradise
, as one “destination”, while another will rank individual towns, beaches or areas that comprise several entries from one country or state, based on their own merit.

Our 10 best places to surf in the world, is a list of 10 surfing destinations that are expansive areas providing  easy access to multiple surf spots, eliminating the need for lengthy car rides between them.

Apart from being famous for their consistent waves, these places also feature in our list of best places to surf in the world even for other several factors, like infrastructure (town, accommodation, restaurants), natural beauty and non-surfing activities — so you can do other things when you are not surfing.
We’re looking for a bit of variety as well as simply an elite 10. The world of surfing is ever-evolving, with new spots continually emerging, while others fade into obscurity due to
issues such as overcrowding
and excessive development.
In no particular order, here’s our list of the top 10 best places to surf in the world!
1. Gold Coast, Australia

Australia’s 6th largest city, Queensland’s surf Mecca is known for its many right-hand point breaks, long beach breaks and a new artificial reef at Narrow Neck. Dry winters, a mild subtropical climate and a massive hinterland of forested, mountainous national parks make this city of nearly 600,000 a pleasant combination of  natural beauty with all the perks and conveniences of a urban surfing destination.
Best Time to go Surfing at Gold Coast:
December to April
Not to be Missed:

Straddie, Snapper (Superbank), The Spit, Main Beach, Greenmount (Coolangatta beach), Surfers Paradise, Currumbin (The Alley), Burleigh, Miami, Duranbah, Broadbeachand Mermaid Beach, Palm Beach, Tugun, Bilinga, and Kirra.
2. Jeffrey’s Bay, South Africa

Home to Supertubes, the longest and world’s best right-hander, according to many experts, “J-Bay” provides a choice of 10 different sections and is popular with high-level surfers, intermediates and longboarders alike. Be careful of the currents, be sure to wear booties and have a strong board.
Best Time to go Surfing at Jeffrey’s Bay:
May to September
Not to be Missed:
Besides the pro-level Supertubes, Jefferey’s Bay’s other sections are comprised of The Point, Magnatubes, Kitchen Windows, Impossibles, Salad Bowls, Boneyards, Coins, Albatross, and Tubes.
3. Bukit Peninsula, Bali, Indonesia
Eat, pray, surf! An amazing cluster of top-level breaks, the northwest coast of the “Bukit” boasts of some of
Bali’s best beaches
and Asia’s premier surf spots with a selection of waves suitable for all levels from absolute beginner (
Padang Padang
Right) to expert and pro (
Uluwatu
, Padang Padang Left). Besides surfing, check out the divine beauty of the Uluwatu
temple

, which overlooks the world-class surf break of the same name. The Bukit is worth exploring, taking in the gorgeous natural scenery along the coast and finding which break is best for you. Visit Bali once and you will know why this place finds itself a place in most of the lists of best places to surf in the world.
Best Time to go Surfing at Bukit Peninsula:
March to November
Not to be Missed:
Other world-famous surf spots on the Bukit include Bingin, Dreamland, Impossibles, and Balangan. Want to learn more? Check out our ‘
10 Best Surf Spots in Bali
‘ guide.
4. Malibu, California, USA
Home to the first dedicated
World Surfing Reserve

, this emblematic corner of Californian culture is famous not only for its waves, but also for appearances in Hollywood films and television series. A great location for beginners and expert surfers alike, Malibu’s proximity to Los Angeles and other SoCal landmarks makes it a perfect surf spot for those in search of a classic adventure on the West Coast of the US. Malibu’s Surfrider Beach is a perfect spot for long boarders and novices, but can be very crowded. In general, California means up to 300 days of fantastic surfing conditions per year. Visiting Malibu is to take pilgrimage to the birth of modern surf culture and the first place surfing took hold outside of its Polynesian birthplace.
Best Time to go Surfing at Malibu:
August and September
Not to be Missed:
Topanga Beach, Surfrider Beach, Zuma Beach and County Line
5. Oahu, Hawaii, USA
Here is where we make an exception and name an entire island as one of our top destinations. In truth it would be a crime to leave Oahu out of any surfing-related top 10 list.
CNN

named Oahu’s Pipeline as the world’s number 1 surf spot, though only experts will be able to ride these legendary 6-meter waves. Fortunately, there are also plenty of other breaks located on the island that is the birthplace of modern surfing.
Best Time to go Surfing at Oahu:
November to March
Not to be Missed:

The famous North Shore is 7 miles of spots including Backdoor, Waimea Bay, Ka’ena Point, Sunset Beach, Pupukea Beach, and Kahuku Point. Learners can also take advantage of the calmer waves at Waikiki.
6. Ericeira, Portugal
Home of the
ASP World Tour Surf Championship
at Ribeira d’Ilhas beach, Ericeira is a spectacular surfing destination by any standard. Due to its 9 top surfing beaches and high environmental standards, in 2011
Ericeira

was made Europe’s first World Surfing Reserve. It is also in close proximity to over 30 other surf spots in the region and a short drive to the Portuguese capital, Lisbon, which is more than worth a few day trips. Ericeira’s 8 miles (5 km) of coastline includes a variety of breaks ranging from those suitable for beginners (Foz do Lizandro, São Julião and Praia do Sul) to experienced and expert surfers (Ribeira, Coxos, Cave, São Lorenzo). Besides waves,
Ericeira
offers the authentic charms of a traditional Portuguese fishing village and the abundant natural scenery the region is famous for.
Best Time to go
Surfing at Ericeira:
May to October
Not to be Missed:
Ericeira’s 8 miles (5 km) of coastline includes a variety of breaks ranging from those suitable for
beginners
(Foz do Lizandro, São Julião and Praia do Sul) to experienced and expert surfers (Ribeira, Coxos, Cave, São Lorenzo).

body.alt: Top 10 surfing destinations in the world
body.caption: Ericeira, pic: Jose Gregorio

children.text: 7. Hossegor, France

Another center for European surfing, Hossegor, situated on southwestern France’s coast of Landes, boasts miles of beautiful beach breaks and some impressive pro-level tubes that are reminiscent of Hawaii’s best offerings. Though mansions dot the coastline, there are plenty of campsites for budget-minded surfers making it a worthy contender for one of the best places to surf in the world
Best Time to go Surfing at Hossegor:
May to November. Each year in September, pros compete at the Quicksilver Pro France at Hossegor.
Not to be Missed:
Les Culs Nus, Hossegor Plage Central, La Graviere, Les Estagnots, Le Penon, and Les Bourdaines.
8. Santa Cruz, California, USA
Ranked number 1 by
Alex Wade

in the London Times, this center of NorCal surfing has everything for every level of surfer and is a living piece of surf history. Beginners will find a home on Cowell’s gentler waves, while experts can test themselves at Steamer Lane’s right-hand point break. For those in between there are a pair of right-handers called Pleasure Point and The Hook. Santa Cruz is also a sizeable town with a nice shopping and restaurant area, summertime boardwalk and a seedier underbelly. The proximity of San Francisco also facilitates day trips to this fantastic city.
Best Time to go Surfing at Santa Cruz:
All year-round
Not to be Missed:
The Hook, Capitola Beach, Cowell’s, Steamer Lane, Monterey, and Manresa Beach
9. Puerto Escondido, Mexico

Looking for top quality barrels, nice beginner waves, great food and beautiful scenery on a budget? Head for Puerto Escondido, the “Mexican Pipeline” on the Pacific coast of Oaxaca State and home to one of the world’s best beach breaks. Hire a cheap beach bungalow and get ready for a great and thrifty surfing holiday. It’s also recommended to drive to different surf spots up and down the coast, using Puerto Escondido as your base.
The Point, Zicatela Beach and Kiddie Break.
Best Time to go Surfing at Puerto Escondido:
March to December
Not to be Missed:
The Point, Zicatela Beach and Kiddie Break.
10. San Juan del Sur,
Nicaragua

This growing tourist hub of Central America offers a wide range of surfing beaches and activities including hiking, dining, nightlife, shopping, yoga and other water and adventure sports like ziplining, sport fishing, biking and sailing. Despite its increasing popularity among the surf community, crowds are still smaller than comparable destinations in Mexico, Costa Rica and El Salvador. A welcoming cosmopolitan town that is popular with expats from the US, San Juan del Sur’s
notable surf spots
for everyone right from beginners to seasoned surfers.
Best Time to go Surfing at San Juan del Sur:
All year round (depending on your level)
Not to be Missed:
Playa Maderas

(with waves suitable for all skill levels), Playa Remanso (perfect for beginners and 10 minutes from town), Playa Yankee (advanced), Colorado (left and right, river-mouth, barrels), Playa Coco (beginners) and Playa Hermosa (1 mile long, boat accessible, occasional barrels and lots of free peaks!).

body.alt: Top 10 surfing destinations in the world
body.caption: San Juan del Sur

Due to its quality spots and incredible swell during summer, people travel to Bali from all over the world. Many return over and over again.

Bali, with its abundant tropical forests, famous waves, and unmatched surfing vibe, stands out as an immensely appealing place for those looking to learn how to surf. No matter your skill level, you’ll leave Bali as a better surfer which is why this place is a must-visit for surfers. As are the sheer amount of
beginner-friendly surf locations
that it offers.
Why is Bali such a popular surfing destination?

One of the best things about surfing in Bali is the variety of breaks, which range from beginner level to professional and “kamikaze”, meaning you’d have to be a bit suicidal to attempt them unless you’re a real expert. Most of these spots are within easy reach of one another, with a famous cluster on the west coast of the Bukit Peninsula.
Surf Bali
from
matthew
on
Vimeo
What is the Best Time for Surfing in Bali?

The island gets the best swell during the dry season between May until September. This is considered the peak surfing season in Bali when the most popular surf breaks located on the west coast of the island hold ideal conditions for surfing. Unfortunately, this is also when the island is most crowded.

The “off-season” (also known as the “Wet season” or “Rainy season”) offers quality surf on the east coast and provides an alternative to the tourist crowds. If you want to avoid crowds and aren’t afraid of a little rain you might want to take advantage of
Bali’s Rainy Season

, which can still produce great waves. Nusa Dua is also a popular beginners wave during the rainy season. Don’t let frightening images of South and Southeast Asian monsoon rains scare you off. Bali’s wet season is considerably tamer, the worst conditions resulting only in minor floods and heavy winds, during which road travel should be avoided.
Where can you Surf in Bali?
For Expert Surfers
The aforementioned Bukit cluster of hot surf spots includes
Uluwatu
(which is actually 5 breaks in one), Impossibles, Bingin, Balangan, Nyang-Nyang, and of course
Padang Padang
. These are mostly pro-level or for highly experienced surfers, but beginners need not fear if they are accompanied by certified instructors.
For Beginners Surfers

Padang Padang Right is probably the best spot for beginners because of its long unbroken wave. Balangan and Dreamland are also suitable for beginners during the right times. Ask one of our helpful surf guides, if you’re not sure where to go.
Other Hidden Gems
While spots like
Padang Padang
and
Uluwatu
are legendary, there are other, lesser-known gems dotting the coastline.
So how to find these hidden surf Bali treasures? Start by doing a little research and when you get to Bali hook up with a
surf camp
or
surf school
. Make sure it’s staffed with qualified instructors who have experience and a deep local knowledge of surfing Bali. Both of Rapture Surfcamps in Bali offers all that.
Things to Know before Planning your first Bali Surf Trip?
Before you book flights to Bali the team here at Rapture has got a few travel tips to make your first surf trip in Indonesia a cracker.
1. Passport and Visa

body.alt: Picture of an Open Passport with Multiple Visa Stamps
body.caption: Avoid last-minute paperwork with early preparation.

children.text: The requirements for Balinese tourist visas can change very fast and vary over a month. This can be confusing when trying to determine if you need a visa for your learn to surf trip in Bali. As a

At the moment there over 160 nationalities that don’t need to pre-apply for a Balinese visa. This means that people from these countries are eligible for a free 30-day visa upon arrival which can then be extended another 30 days at an Indonesian immigration office or via a local travel agent. The penalty fee for overstaying your visa is IDR 1,000,000 per day, which works out to be about USD 70. Your passport must be valid for at least 6 months from the day of your arrival.
2. Book Transport from Airport before your Reach

body.caption:
Organize transport in Bali with us and start your vacation with zero stress

When you step out of the airport, you’ll be welcomed with a bunch of taxi drivers waiting for customers. They’ll offer to help you with you things and claim that they can take you anywhere on the island for the best rates. While there may be some genuine ones but most of the time, these drivers are just taking advantage of you how tired you are to charge you exorbitantly.

We advice that you plan your transportation from the airport in advance to avoid paying higher rates than needed.

children.text: 3. Don’t Forget Travel Insurance

body.alt: Picture of evening skies and ocean
body.caption: If you have an accident and aren’t covered by travel insurance, you’ll be cursing yourself out twice… one time for all the money lost and a second time for all the waves you’ll miss

children.text: If you still think travel insurance is an unnecessary expense, you’re kidding yourself. However, this goes double if you’re a surfer. With
travel insurance
, you can hit the waves with peace of mind and actually enjoy your
surf trip
without having to worry about what you would do if things go pearshaped.

Protect yourself and your peace of mind by getting an insurance for your lost/stolen luggage and flight changes. Most of these travel insurance plans also cover any unexpected medical costs you may incur abroad.
Think about this – a medical emergency in your home country may not seem like a big deal but if it were to happen in Bali or any other country, it can end up being expense.
When browsing through travel insurance place, look for any
surfer-friendly ones
. Something that would cover surfboard apart from the usual medical and baggage.
4. Understand Local Currency and Conversion

body.alt: Picture of Indonesian Currency
body.caption: Monopoly money or Rupiah?

children.text: It is crucial to have knowledge about the local currency and the exchange rate in relation to your own currency when you decide to learn how to surf in Bali. The Indonesian currency, known as Rupiah or Rp., appears distinct from various other currencies, making it challenging to comprehend. To avoid the inevitable situation of relying on ATMs at the airport, which can be overwhelming when you are already dealing with jetlag, it is recommended to exchange some cash before your departure. This way, you can be better prepared and have one less thing to worry about.

Once you’ve reached your accommodation, ask your fellow guests for a nearby, reliable money changer. Some people may suggest changing your money at the airport upon arrival, which is a good option for small amounts. However, better rates can be found in town. If you’re unsure, feel free to talk to our friendly staff members at Rapture Surfcamps.
5. Let go of your Fear of ‘Bali Belly’

body.alt: Chicken Satay - a skewered chicken dish

children.text: Don’t worry about getting sick on your learn to surf trip to Bali! The “Bali belly” is not as common as it used to be. In the past, certain foods were considered risky, but hygiene standards have improved with the increase in tourists. You can now find delicious and healthy options in almost every café and restaurant. There might be an occasional bad meal, but it’s rare. Just be cautious of the tap water. If you do get sick, seek a doctor to take antibiotics and drink lots of water. You’ll likely feel better within 24 hours.
6. Check out and Book Surf Camps before you Arrive

body.alt: Picture of an ocean taken from a cliff.
body.caption: Why wouldn’t anyone want to reserve a surf camp close to such a stunning location?

children.text: Interested in reserving a surf journey to Bali but unsure of where to stay, how to rent a surfboard, or where to find the best waves? No need to worry. If you organize your stay with
Rapture Surfcamps in Bali
, we will take care of all your surfing requirements and more. Enjoy airport shuttle to Yoga sessions, at our surf camps in Bali!

Our camps are available throughout the year and we can also arrange excursions to various destinations in Bali, such as Ubud, Kintamani, and Tanelot. Furthermore, our surf guides are well-versed in Bali’s surf spots and can assist you in finding the perfect waves. They will take you to the top breaks and ensure you get in the water before the crowds arrive. By booking a surf camp in advance, you can relieve yourself of the stress of arranging everything upon arrival. Taking you to all the best breaks and getting you in the line-up
before the crowds arrive

By booking a surf camp before you arrive, you’ll take a lot of the pressure of sorting everything out upon landing. You can then simply roll up and kick back and let us do all the hard work. While you make the most of your learn to surf trip in Bali.
7. Research a Few Flat-day Activities

body.alt: Mount Batur with clouds around it.
body.caption: Watching the sunrise from Mount Batur is something you’ll remember for the rest of your life

children.text: There will be occasions when the breeze is blowing towards the shore, the waves are coming from the opposite direction, or you’re simply exhausted from surfing and couldn’t paddle for more than 10 meters without dozing off. Such days present an ideal chance to
explore the other captivating parts
of this exquisite island.

Indulge in a rejuvenating Balinese massage to refresh your body, or for a more adventurous experience, you can opt for the thrilling activity of whitewater rafting on the Ayung River. If you prefer to stay in the water but aren’t keen on surfing, there are numerous opportunities to explore snorkeling and diving.

Before you make reservations for a surf camp in Bali, make sure to explore some alternative options for when the waves are not suitable for surfing.The pristine reefs of Menjangan, in particular, remain untouched. Nusa Lembongan is renowned globally for its flourishing marine ecosystem. Other attractions that may interest the inquisitive traveler include exploring the Jatiluwih rice terraces, ascending the volcanic Mount Batur, and embarking on countryside tours. globally for its abundant marine life. Other activities that might attract the adventurous traveler include taking rides through the picturesque Jatiluwih rice fields, hiking up the volcanic Mount Batur, and going on countryside tours.
8. Brush up on your Bahasa

body.alt: Three smiling kids standing on the street.
body.caption: Making friends is easy when you know the lingo

children.text: Familiarizing yourself with the local language can facilitate building new relationships. It may come as a surprise, but Balinese Indonesian possesses certain unique characteristics that set it apart from standard Indonesian. Known as Bahasa, this variant eliminates verb conjugations and tenses, making it a more straightforward language to grasp compared to most romance languages.

Known as Bahasa, Balinese Indonesian does not require verb conjugations or tenses, making it more accessible to learn compared to many romance languages. Nevertheless, it does entail different social differentiations influenced by a caste system, which is crucial to grasp if one wishes to truly master Bahasa. It is always commendable when visitors make an effort to communicate in the language of the country they are visiting, even if it is only a few words.
Check out
this handy guide
for an introduction to Balinese if this sounds like you. If you’d prefer to speak in English though that’s also not a problem. The vast majority of Balinese speak enough English to converse with you.

body.alt: A girl holding a yellow surf board with Rapture written on it
body.caption: Surfing in Bali with Rapture Surfcamps

children.text: Additional surf travel tips for Bali
When you’re in the water, remember to smile and be friendly. But be careful not to smile too much, as people might think you’re up to something.
When you go out partying at night, only bring your cash and maybe your phone. Bali is notorious for causing even the best ravers to stumble.
Always wear a helmet when riding your bike.
Drink plenty of Bintangs, but avoid Arak.
Step out of your comfort zone and make new friends.
Remember to pack earplugs, cable ties, and Vaseline for rashes.
Don’t rely solely on the swell forecast, go check the waves yourself.
Pack for humid weather, but also bring trainers for hiking or riding. If you’re a female, don’t forget to bring a sarong.
Always make sure your taxi driver has turned on the fare meter.
Don’t be afraid to try the local cuisine. We can’t stress it enough, homemade nasi goreng is life.

What is the best time of year to visit Bali?
The optimal time to visit Bali is during the dry season, from April to October. This period offers pleasant weather, lower humidity, and ideal conditions for exploring the island.
Is it necessary to get any vaccinations before traveling to Bali?

It’s advisable to consult with a healthcare professional to determine the necessary vaccinations before traveling to Bali. Typically, vaccinations for Hepatitis A and B, typhoid, and routine childhood immunizations are recommended.
What clothing is appropriate to wear in Bali?

Bali has a warm and tropical climate, so lightweight and breathable clothing is recommended. Modesty is appreciated in temples and more rural areas, so it’s advisable to cover shoulders and knees when visiting such places.
Are there any cultural norms or customs I should be aware of in Bali?

Yes, Bali has a rich cultural heritage, and it’s important to respect local customs. Balinese people are generally friendly, but it’s customary to greet with a smile and a nod. When entering a temple, wearing a sarong and sash is a sign of respect.
What are the transportation options in Bali?

Bali offers various transportation options, including taxis, ride-sharing services, and scooter rentals. For longer distances, private drivers and rented cars are popular choices. Traffic can be congested, especially in tourist-heavy areas.
Is it safe to drink tap water in Bali?

It’s advisable to drink bottled or filtered water in Bali to avoid the risk of waterborne illnesses. Many hotels and restaurants provide complimentary bottled water, and it’s widely available for purchase.
Are there any specific safety precautions for beach activities in Bali?

While Bali’s beaches are beautiful, it’s important to be cautious of strong currents, especially during the monsoon season. Always swim in designated areas, pay attention to warning flags, and be aware of your surroundings.
What are the currency and payment methods commonly used in Bali?

The official currency in Bali is the Indonesian Rupiah (IDR). Cash is widely accepted, especially in local markets, but credit cards are commonly used in larger establishments and hotels. It’s advisable to have some cash for smaller transactions.
Are there any health and safety concerns for food in Bali?

Bali offers a diverse culinary scene, and most food is safe to eat. However, it’s recommended to choose restaurants and street food vendors with good hygiene practices. Peel fruits and vegetables, drink bottled beverages, and avoid undercooked food.
How can I stay connected in Bali?

Bali has good internet connectivity, and most hotels, restaurants, and cafes offer Wi-Fi. Additionally, you can purchase local SIM cards for your phone to have data access during your stay. Ensure that your phone is unlocked to use a local SIM card.

What does the rental include?

At the moment you can only use the equipment you have booked. Nevertheless, Awayco soon will have a solution so you can switch your equipment.
Which type of boards can I rent at Padang surf camp?

body.alt: Awayco Inventory Bali Padang

children.text: How are the prices of the rental determined?
Daily prices vary based on inventory and demand on a specific location, but the longer you book the gear, the lower the day-rate will be the price of the rental.
What happens if I bring the equipment back late?

Keep in mind that if you bring back the gear too late, you will be charged a late fee. This fee represents 2x the price of the first day of the rental. For example, if your first day is USD 20, the late fee is USD 40.
What if the surf equipment I rented gets damaged?
Whenever you reserve a product you can add product damage coverage to the gear you rent. The two levels of product damage coverage offered by Awayco are:
Basic product damage coverage that will cover any minor and medium damage to the gear rented
Premium product damage coverage that will extend the cover also to major damage including damage beyond repair
Although
Awayco
is covering minor/moderate damage to any rented equipment, if the gear is severely damaged and with no room to repair, Flex members will be charged USD 300 for the replacement cost.
On the other side, Prime Members don’t need to pay anything.
In case I can’t pick up my gear how does the cancellation fee work?
In the case that your gear isn’t picked up by the end of the day of your 1st booking day, it will automatically be canceled, and you will be charged.
If you are a Flex member and want to cancel your booking, you will have to do it 48h before the starting date. If you don’t do this, you will be charged for the first day of your booking.
As a Prime member, you can cancel up to 24h prior, for free.
If there are any delays, please contact
Rapture Surfcamps Padang
and let our staff know. We will always try to make the best arrangements for you.
